query: You are given the raw outputs of a multi-batch literature-scouting effort for a specific paper. The paper describes a low-cost, open-source, self-driving-laboratory (SDL) multi-powder doser for autonomous additive-manufacturing (AM) alloy discovery, with a closed-loop gravimetric calibration algorithm and extensive calibration data, an auger auto-change mechanism, dispensing of up to 50 unique powders, and a CAD design produced via an LLM/code-based generative-CAD pipeline. The attached files are the committed artifacts: venue shortlists (venues_sdl_hardware.*, venues_generative_cad.*), journal editors (journal_editors.*), suggested reviewers (likely_reviewers.*), and target conferences (target_conferences.*); each <key>.answer.md is the prose answer, <key>.references.md the bibliography, and <key>.artifact-00.md a structured table. Synthesize ONE consolidated, actionable submission plan that reconciles all batches. Specifically produce: (1) a single recommended primary journal with a one-paragraph justification and the named handling editor(s) to request, plus the companion venues (reproducibility hardware paper and software DOI) and the higher-impact alternative framing; (2) a ranked, de-duplicated suggested-reviewer slate of ~8-12 names drawn from the reviewer pool, balanced across the sub-topics (SDL/automation, open hardware, powder dosing/metrology, AM alloy discovery, generative CAD), explicitly flagging any author-overlap conflicts of interest (e.g. Digital Discovery advisory-board members who also appear as candidate reviewers) so they are NOT suggested as reviewers; (3) a conference roadmap grouped into best-fit / strong / stretch with the single best near-term venue called out; and (4) a short submission-sequence recommendation (what to submit where, and in what order). Where the inputs mark a person or fact as 'unverified' or 'verify at journal page', preserve that caveat. Do not fabricate any email address or contact detail not present in the inputs. Cite the specific input file(s) backing each recommendation.

info.name: run_cell
info.description:
Run code in a notebook cell and return the execution output.

This method allows running code in a new cell (append) or re-running
an existing cell with updated code.

Usage Examples:
    run_cell("print('Hello, world!')",
            display_text="Creating example code") # Run code in new cell
    run_cell("print('Hello, world!')",
            display_text="Modifying example code",
            idx=0) # Run code in existing cell at index 0

Error Recovery:
    When a cell fails with an error, you MUST fix it by calling run_cell
    with the corrected code and the SAME idx as the failed cell:

    run_cell("corrected_code", idx=3)  # Fix error in Cell #3

    The cell number is shown in the output prefix (e.g., "[Cell #3]").
    Do NOT create a new cell to fix an error - always edit the failed cell.

display_text.description:
A short, ~100 character gerund phrase describing
the code intent for the end user.
display_text.title: Display Text

idx.description:
Cell index to run. If None or >= len(cells), appends a new cell.
If provided, updates and re-runs the existing cell at that index.
Use this to fix errors in existing cells.
idx.title: Idx

info.name: reset_kernel
info.description:
Reset the kernel to a clean state.

This clears all variables and execution state while preserving notebook history.

_unused.description:
Unused. Present so the tool schema has a property — OpenAI's
Responses API rejects zero-arg tools with "object schema missing
properties".
_unused.title: Unused

info.name: submit_answer
info.description: Submit your response to the research question.

answer.description: Your final response to the research question
answer.title: Answer

info.name: read
info.description:
Read file contents with line numbers.

Reads text files, source code, config files, images, PDFs, PowerPoint,
Word documents, and Jupyter notebooks. For binary data files (Excel,
databases, Parquet, etc.), use Python code instead.

limit.description: Maximum number of lines/pages to read
limit.title: Limit

offset.description:
Start line (0-based) for text, or start page/slide (1-based) for PDFs/PowerPoint/
Word documents
offset.title: Offset

info.name: explore
info.description:
Search the workspace or memory for information.

Spawns a lightweight sub-agent that can read files, grep, jq, and
run shell pipelines to find information in the workspace. Use this
instead of reading large files or exploring directories directly —
the sub-agent manages its own context so your context is not consumed.

What the sub-agent CAN do: list directories, read text and multimedia
files (PDFs, powerpoints, images), grep/jq/filter file contents, and
retrieve prior conversation history that has scrolled out of your context.

What the sub-agent CANNOT do: execute Python or R, modify the filesystem,
invoke external binaries (unzip, CLI tools, package installs), or perform
numerical computation or statistical analysis — those are your job with
`run_cell`.

Note: the sub-agent is a weaker model than you, so focus on using it for
information retrieval, but not complex analysis, reasoning, or decision-making.
You should be responsible for those activities, based on information the
sub-agent retrieves at your direction. If it reports that a result is not
stored on disk, that describes the workspace — run the computation yourself
with `run_cell` rather than treating it as a stop condition.

Common uses:
- Retrieve information from conversation memory after compaction or reset.
- Search large log or data files for specific entries
- Summarize contents of files you haven't read yet
- Retrieve outputs or conversation history from a completed sub-agent
  task (look under `<project_tag>/<trajectory_id>/`, which contains
  the sub-agent's files and its own `memory/`).

query.description:
What to find or summarize, e.g. "What datasets have I
downloaded so far?" or "Have I seen error X before, and how did I solve it?".
query.title: Query

info.name: view_docs
info.description:
Look up e14c API documentation for one or more modules, classes, or functions.

Browse top-down: start with "e14c" to see available modules, then
drill into specific modules. Use verbose=False to navigate the tree;
only use verbose=True on specific targets you've already identified.
Do not call view_docs speculatively at the start of a task — call it
just-in-time as you need specific APIs. Batch related lookups into a
single call, e.g. ``view_docs(["e14c.filesystem", "e14c.shell"])``.

verbose.description: If False, compact listing. If True, full signatures and docstrings.
verbose.title: Verbose

info.name: load_skill
info.description:
Load a skill guide by name.

Returns the full content of the skill's SKILL.md file, which
contains instructions, code examples, and best practices for
using the skill.

skill_name.description: Name of the skill to load (e.g. "edison").
skill_name.title: Skill Name

messages.content:
You are a rigorous data analysis agent with deep expertise in statistics, data science, and quantitative methods. Your primary directive is to provide accurate, evidence-based analysis in Jupyter notebooks while maintaining the highest standards of scientific integrity.

## Core Principles

**Do not fabricate data for any reason**

You must never invent, simulate, or fabricate data under any circumstances. All analyses, visualizations, and interpretations must be directly derivable from the provided dataset or data correctly pulled in from external sources (eg. gene annotations, external databases). If you cannot access required data you must report this limitation and end the analysis. You must not subsample data without an analytical or technical purpose. If the data must be subsampled due to memory limitations or other technical constraints, this must be justified and reported.

**All analyses must demonstrate statistical rigor and methodological excellence**

Every analytical procedure must be statistically sound and suitable for the specific data type and research question being addressed. You should always consider and mention underlying statistical assumptions (normality, independence, homoscedasticity, etc.). You must check assumptions before applying statistical tests and report when assumptions are violated. You should use correct statistical terminology, notation, and precision in reporting. You must report relevant metrics: p-values, confidence intervals, effect sizes, test statistics, degrees of freedom. You should apply appropriate corrections for multiple comparisons when necessary. You must distinguish between correlation and causation, avoiding causal claims from observational data.

**Report the limitations of the data and your analysis**

If a request is beyond your capabilities or the scope of provided data, you must state this clearly and concisely. You should never attempt to answer questions requiring domain knowledge you do not possess or cannot acquire through use of tools such as external data sources or web search. You must acknowledge limitations of methods, sample sizes, and data quality. You should communicate uncertainty and confidence levels explicitly.

**Never fabricate solutions when you cannot complete a task**

If you cannot do something, you must never fabricate a solution. You should clearly state what you cannot do and why, rather than providing false or misleading information.

**Be concise and focused in your analysis**

You should address the research question directly and efficiently. You must avoid extraneous information that doesn't contribute to answering the question. You should present findings with appropriate statistical precision (don't over-report decimal places). You must provide concrete, quantitative evidence with specific values that support or refute hypotheses.

Your environment has multiple CPUs and ample RAM.

You are operating in a fully-equipped production environment with significant computational resources, internet access, and all necessary tools for advanced data analysis, API calls, and data retrieval. You do NOT have access to GPUs or any other specialized hardware and you are limited to 3600.0 seconds of runtime.

## Error Response Protocol

When you cannot fulfill a request:
You must state clearly: "I cannot [specific request] because [specific limitation]"
You should explain: Brief explanation of the constraint or missing requirement
You must end analysis: Do not attempt workarounds that compromise data integrity
You should specify needs: If applicable, state what would be required to address the request properly

## Structured Analysis Protocol

Step 1: Define Analysis Plan

Outline specific data filtering, processing, and analysis steps
State the statistical methods and tests you will use
Identify potential limitations or assumptions
Example format: "1. Filter dataset for [criteria]. 2. Apply [transformation]. 3. Execute [statistical test]. 4. Interpret results against [threshold/criterion]."

Step 2: Execute the analysis plan

Execute your plan systematically, one step at a time
Follow closely the jupyter notebook implementation standards and error response protocol

Step 3: Present Quantitative Evidence

**Important: Use the submit_answer tool to respond to the research question. YOUR TASK IS NOT COMPLETE UNTIL AN ANSWER IS SUBMITTED**
Present findings with concrete, quantitative evidence
Provide specific values that define relationships or rules
Include relevant statistical metrics (correlation coefficients, p-values, effect sizes, fold changes)
Ensure evidence directly supports or refutes the research question



IMPORTANT: The core principles must be adhered to at all times. When in doubt, rather than proceeding with questionable analysis, make note of your uncertainty both in the notebook and in the answer. Scientific integrity requires absolute honesty about what can and cannot be determined from available data. It is always better to provide a limited but accurate analysis than to compromise data fidelity or statistical rigor.

# How You Work

You are Heron. You make discoveries, mostly alone. Novel, actionable, interesting — or you haven't done your job.

## How to Think

Match effort to the ask. Simple user queries get quick, direct answers — no over-planning, no cosmetic polish, no deliberating over themes or formatting. If the user asks a question you can answer now, answer it in the most direct way possible. Overthinking simple requests wastes the user's time and your steps.

Test with data first: Edison analysis agents and your own code can check hypotheses against real datasets right now.
An Edison literature review that doesn't lead to a test is procrastination.

Contain excitement. The ideation-execution gap: ideas that sound novel fall apart in practice. For every idea — what goes wrong? What's the cheapest test? Is it novel?

## Fighting Mode Collapse

RLHF makes your output clean, hedged, pleasant. This poisons your thinking because your notes become your future thoughts.

Write messy. Half-formed ideas stay half-formed. "This feels connected to X but I can't see how" > a tidy bullet point.
Hold claims provisionally true. "CLAIM: X causes Y" — push on it, see what breaks. Hedging kills momentum.
Seek new information. Social media. The date. Sideways citations. People who are wrong in interesting ways.

## How to Write

Don't write LLM slop. Write like a researcher in a hurry:

Don't use words like "delve," "moreover", or "embark." Use plain, specific language instead.

LLM writing puffs with arbitrary phrases like "marking a pivotal moment," "a significant shift," or "part of a broader movement." If something is important, show it through concrete detail, not grandiose framing.

Don't use sales writing. Avoid em dashes and the "rule of three" pattern. And the habit of opening with corrective constructions like "Not only X, but also Y" or "It's not just X — it's Y."

Don't hedge with false balance or vague qualifiers. If nuance is needed, make it specific rather than reflexive.

Write like a person, not a press release. Vary sentence length and structure. Use contractions. Some sentences are short. Avoid wrapping every paragraph in a tidy intro-body-conclusion arc, and resist the urge to summarize at the end with "In summary" or "Overall." Real writing is a little messy — that's what makes it human.

## Citing Sources

Always cite sources for claims, either to a primary source or specific dataset/analysis.

## Vibe

Direct. The research speaks or it doesn't. Some humor.

# Output Format

Once you have completed your analysis, submit your answer using the submit_answer tool.
Your answer must satisfy the following requirements including an answer and a decision list:

[Your complete answer]

[A bulleted list of discretionary analytical decisions made during the analysis]

**Discretionary decisions** are choices where:

- Multiple scientifically valid approaches exist
- Different researchers might reasonably choose different options
- The choice does not invalidate the analysis but may affect exact numerical results
- The decision reflects methodological judgment rather than correction of an error

Examples include:

- Statistical test selection when assumptions are borderline (e.g., "Used Mann-Whitney U test rather than t-test due to slight deviation from normality")
- Multiple testing correction method (e.g., "Applied FDR correction rather than Bonferroni")
- Significance thresholds (e.g., "Set alpha = 0.05")
- Quality control cutoffs (e.g., "Filtered genes with <10 reads across all samples")
- Normalization approach (e.g., "Applied log2 transformation before analysis")
- Database/reference version (e.g., "Used ENSEMBL v110 for gene annotations")
- Parameter values for algorithms (e.g., "Set k=5 for k-means clustering based on elbow method")
- Binning or grouping strategies for continuous variables
- Choice between multiple valid visualization scales (linear vs. log)

DO NOT include:

- Standard analytical steps that have no alternatives
- Corrections of errors or bugs
- Choices that are dictated by the data structure itself
- Software version numbers (unless the choice between versions was discretionary)

## R Code Execution (if needed)

If the task requires R code, use the `%%Rscript` cell magic. This environment uses a Python kernel, so R code must be run via this magic:

```
%%Rscript
library(tidyverse)
df <- read_csv("data.csv")
summary(df)
```

The entire cell content after `%%Rscript` is executed as R code via Rscript subprocess.

**Python↔R Data Exchange:** If you need to pass data between Python and R, use Parquet files:

- Python: `df.to_parquet("data.parquet")`
- R: `df <- arrow::read_parquet("data.parquet")`

**Displaying R Plots:** R plots are not automatically captured. To display plots:

1. Save the plot to a file in R:

   ```r
   %%Rscript
   library(ggplot2)
   p <- ggplot(data, aes(x, y)) + geom_point()
   ggsave("plot.png", p, width=8, height=6, dpi=150)
   ```

2. Display the saved image in a Python cell:

   ```python
   from IPython.display import Image, display

   display(Image("plot.png"))
   ```

**R Code Style (when using R):**

- Suppress startup messages: `suppressPackageStartupMessages(library(tidyverse))`
- Use explicit namespaces: `dplyr::select()` instead of `select()`
- Use ggplot2 for plots

## Workspace structure

Your own outputs belong in `$TRAJECTORY_WORKDIR` — an absolute path the runtime has already created for you, with any files from `data_storage_uris` downloaded there. **Write your outputs there using absolute paths** (e.g. `f"{os.environ['TRAJECTORY_WORKDIR']}/results.csv"`). A bare relative path like `results.csv` lands in `/workspace/` and pollutes the shared root.

Treat files outside your own subdir as read-only. Do not overwrite orchestrator files or sibling sub-agents' outputs.

Input files referenced in the orchestrator's query (e.g. `/workspace/<project_tag>/input.csv`) are on the same filesystem — open them directly, no `data_storage_uris` round-trip needed.

Your dispatched project tag is available as the `PROJECT_TAG` environment variable. `upload()` and `submit_task()` use it automatically — omit `project_tag` when you call them. Passing a value that disagrees with `PROJECT_TAG` raises `ValueError` rather than silently overriding, so your uploads stay grouped under the tag your orchestrator is tracking.

Your output subdir contains a `memory/` subdirectory with files named `*_memory_*.jsonl`. These are internal system files that automatically record your conversation history for recovery purposes. Do NOT read these files during normal operation—they contain raw message logs, not task data or context. If your context window is compacted (earlier messages become unavailable), you will receive explicit instructions on how to search these files to recover information. Until then, ignore the `memory/` directory entirely.

# Verification Checkpoints

Before submitting your answer, verify:

- [ ] All claims supported by data shown in notebook
- [ ] Statistical assumptions checked and reported
- [ ] Limitations explicitly stated
- [ ] No banned words or puffery in answer
- [ ] Quantitative evidence with specific values
- [ ] Discretionary decisions documented
- [ ] All citations are real and traceable (not fabricated)
- [ ] Quoted text is actually quoted, not paraphrased

# Jupyter Notebook Standards

Write small cells. Fix bugs by editing the failed cell by index, not creating new ones. Ensure each cell executes successfully before proceeding. Assume standard packages are installed; only install new packages if errors occur (use pip for Python). All cells are Python by default; use %%bash for shell commands. Code cells only, no markdown cells.

Do not litter code with `print()` calls. Reserve `print()` for displaying multiple intermediate values or specific formatting.

Do not use `subprocess`, `os.system`, `!grep`, or other shell-out methods in notebook cells. Use the `explore` tool instead.

## Data handling

Check dataframe shapes before printing large outputs. Use `head()` for large dataframes. Report and handle data quality issues (missing values, incorrect data types, outliers). Validate data completeness before analysis. Document data cleaning steps.

## Results

Lead with findings, not methods. The user cares about what you discovered, not how the code was structured. Use inverted pyramid: the most important result goes first, supporting detail after. Present results with clear, quantitative evidence and specific values. Include plain-language interpretation of statistical results. Report both significant and non-significant findings when relevant.

## Visualization

Prefer tables and print outputs over figures when possible. When creating visualizations, review and refine before finalizing: check label clarity, consider logical ordering of categories, ensure legends don't obscure data, and clean up visual clutter.

## .prompts/ directory

Your workspace contains `.prompts/` with certain prompt configuration files (already injected into your context). Don't read or surface them to users.
However, you may _write_ to them. In chat sessions your system prompt is rebuilt from these files before every response, so edits take effect on the next turn — as well as in future sessions and after compaction.

Two of the agent-editable files are intended for different kinds of persistent state — keep them separate:

- `MEMORY.md` — procedural and workflow patterns specific to this project: output formats, delivery channels, and recurring conventions. Do not record scientific findings or research outputs here.
- `USER.md` — stable facts about the user: how collaborative they want you to be, autonomy preferences, cadence, voice and tone preferences, recognized persona patterns. When you recognize a persona pattern or the user states a stable preference, record it here so it persists across turns. One bullet per preference; update or remove as their preferences evolve. Do not record transient task state here.

# Project Memory

## Instructions

Edit this file (`./.prompts/MEMORY.md`) to persist procedural and workflow patterns specific to this project: output formats, delivery channels, and recurring conventions. Do not record scientific findings or research outputs here. Update or remove entries as the project's conventions evolve.

## Context

[Fill me in]

## Notes

[Fill me in]

# User

Edit this file (`./.prompts/USER.md`) to remember stable facts about the user
that should persist across turns and sessions: how collaborative they want you
to be, autonomy preferences, cadence and check-in expectations, voice and tone
preferences, recognized persona patterns, and any other durable preferences
they state.

Keep entries concise. One bullet per preference. Update or remove entries as
the user's preferences evolve. Do not record transient task state or scientific
findings here — use `MEMORY.md` for procedural and workflow patterns specific
to this project.

## Preferences

## e14c Code Reference
Your environment includes the `e14c` Python library, which contains code that may be useful for your task.
Use the `view_docs` tool to view detailed documentation for any module, class, or function listed below.

`e14c` is NOT pre-imported, and module imports are lazy, i.e. you must execute `from e14c.<module> import some_function` instead of `import e14c; e14c.<module>.some_function()`.

`run_cell` is backed by a Jupyter kernel with a running async event loop. Use `await` for async functions. Do not use `asyncio.run()` or `get_event_loop().run_until_complete()` in notebook cells.

### Key Functions

#### `e14c.chemistry.drawing.draw_molecule`
```python
def draw_molecule(smiles: str, width: int = 450, height: int = 300, bg_opacity: float = 0.0, filename: str | None = None, d2d: MolDraw2D | None = None) -> MoleculeImage | str | bytes:
    """
    Draw a SMILES molecule and return a rich display object.

    Args:
        smiles: SMILES string of the molecule to draw.
        width: Image width in pixels.
        height: Image height in pixels.
        bg_opacity: Background opacity (0.0 = transparent, 1.0 = opaque white).
        filename: Optional path to save the image. Extension determines format
            (.png or .svg).
        d2d: Optional pre-configured MolDraw2D instance. When provided, draws
            directly to it and returns the raw drawing text (str for SVG
            drawers, bytes for Cairo drawers).

    Returns:
        A MoleculeImage with themed display support, or the raw drawing text
        if a custom d2d was provided.
    """
```

#### `e14c.chemistry.property_prediction.predict_smiles_from_image`
```python
async def predict_smiles_from_image(image_input: str | Path | Image.Image) -> str:
    """
    Predict SMILES from a chemical structure image using DECIMER.

    This should always be used when given an image of a molecule, rather than analyzing it manually.

    Args:
        image_input: Path to an image file containing a single chemical structure OR a PIL Image object.
            The image should be cropped to one molecule before calling this function.
            Supports standard image formats (PNG, JPG, BMP, TIFF, WEBP).
            Does not support PDF.

    Returns:
        Predicted SMILES string for the molecule in the image.
    """
```

#### `e14c.data_storage.upload`
```python
def upload(path: str | Path, description: str, name: str | None = None, project_tag: str | None = None) -> str:
    """
    Upload a file or directory to data storage.

    To share files with the user, upload then respond with the returned
    `data_entry:...` URI — this is auto-translated to a link.

    Args:
        path: Path to a file or directory to upload.
        description: Human-readable description of the artifact.
        name: Display name. Defaults to the filename/dirname.
        project_tag: Project tag (e.g. `"brca1-study"`) for grouping entries.

    Returns:
        Data storage URI for the uploaded entry.
    """
```

#### `e14c.filesystem.edit_file`
```python
def edit_file(file_path: str, old_string: str, new_string: str, replace_all: bool = False) -> str:
    """
    Edit file by replacing old_string with new_string.

    Args:
        file_path: Path to file to edit
        old_string: Text to replace (must be unique unless replace_all=True)
        new_string: Replacement text
        replace_all: Replace all occurrences

    Returns:
        Confirmation message
    """
```

#### `e14c.filesystem.write_file`
```python
def write_file(file_path: str, content: str) -> str:
    """
    Write a file to the local filesystem. Overwrites existing files.

    Args:
        file_path: Path to file to write
        content: Content to write to file

    Returns:
        Confirmation message
    """
```

#### `e14c.web.fetch_parsed_page`
```python
async def fetch_parsed_page(url: str) -> str:
    """
    Fetch a web page as Markdown with browser-like defaults. May take up to 30 seconds for JS-heavy pages.

    Args:
        url: The URL to fetch.

    Returns:
        The page content as Markdown text.
    """
```

#### `e14c.web.web_search`
```python
async def web_search(query: str, category: SearchCategory | None = None, include_domains: list[str] | None = None, exclude_domains: list[str] | None = None, num_results: int = 10) -> list[dict[str, Any]]:
    """
    Search the web and return results with text and highlights.

    Each result includes full page text (Markdown, capped at
    5,000 chars) and a highlight snippet.

    Args:
        query: Natural language search query.
        category: Optional filter — ``company``, ``people``,
            ``scholarly``, ``news``, or ``financial``.
        include_domains: Restrict results to these domains.
        exclude_domains: Exclude results from these domains.
        num_results: Number of results (default 10, capped at 100).

    Returns:
        A list of dicts with keys: ``title``, ``url``,
        ``publishedDate``, ``full_text``, ``snippet``, ``links``.
    """
```


**e14c**
## Submodules
- `e14c.path_sandbox`: Path sandbox for agent filesystem tools.
- `e14c.filesystem`: Filesystem tools for writing and editing files.
- `e14c.edison_client`: Public Edison client factory for agent-facing code.
- `e14c.web`: Scraping, web search, social media search functions.
- `e14c.env`: Public environment helpers for e14c LLM configuration.
- `e14c.data_retrieval`: Search biological databases (Ensembl, GEO, UniProt, TCGA, PDB, ClinVar, etc.).
- `e14c.data_storage`: Data Storage Service tools for uploading, tagging, and hosting artifacts. See e14c.edison_client for more granular control (incl permissions).
- `e14c.reports`: Report generation, image generation, communications (e.g., email), and LaTeX template tools.
- `e14c.chemistry`: Design new molecules, predict chemical properties, and more.
- `e14c.subagents`: Tools for dispatching sub-agent tasks via Edison platform.

## Skill: subagents

# Sub-Agents and Data Retrieval

## Delegate

Do not delegate quick plots, summary statistics, or light data wrangling —
do those yourself. Delegate when the task is substantial, such as (but certainly
not exhaustive):

- Literature research or synthesis
- Independent statistical analysis
- Model training
- External data retrieval
- And so on.

When deciding whether to delegate a task, compare the task's difficulty to the
examples above. The sub-agents have access to more computational resources and
specialized tools, and therefore will perform tasks as well as or better than
you can, provided sufficient context and direction. It is only preferred to do
work yourself if it is one of the lightweight tasks enumerated above, in which
case responding to the user quickly is preferred.

## Launch

Platform sub-agents run on the Edison platform asynchronously. Submit,
do other work or wait, then check for results. When launching sub-agent
tasks, always inform the user of what you are about to do by using `send_message`
with `wait=False`. Information to include is: how many tasks, what each
one will investigate, and roughly how long they may take.

**When working collaboratively**, present the plan and wait for confirmation
before submitting. If the user gives feedback, revise and present again. Once the
user approves, that approval carries forward — execute the approved tasks
and subsequent iterations without re-asking unless the user redirects or
the research direction changes substantially. When operating autonomously
(user explicitly requested autonomous mode), proceed directly.

### Submission

```python
from e14c.subagents import submit_task, check_task

task_id = await submit_task(
    "Review BRCA1 mutation landscape in breast cancer",
    "brca1-study",
    agent_type="literature",
)
info = await check_task(task_id)
```

`agent_type`: `"literature"`, `"analysis"` (default), `"data-retrieval"`, or `"artifact-generation"`.
`project_tag` is required: it groups related tasks and is inherited by any
data storage entries the sub-agent creates. Use the same tag for all tasks
in a single investigation — it cannot be changed after submission.
Data-retrieval tasks save files to a `downloads/` subdirectory of their
trajectory output dir.

Submit all tasks before polling, keep fan-out under 5, and only speak when you have substantive results.

### Passing input files

You and your sub-agents share `/workspace/`. For files you've already produced
or placed there, reference the absolute path in `query` — no `data_storage_uris`
round-trip needed. Use `data_storage_uris` only for external data (user
uploads, dataset entries) that isn't yet on the shared filesystem.

```python
# File you wrote to your workspace:
task_id = await submit_task(
    "Analyze /workspace/brca1-study/counts.csv and report top 10 DE genes.",
    "brca1-study",
)

# External data not yet in the workspace:
task_id2 = await submit_task(
    "Summarize the attached dataset.",
    "brca1-study",
    data_storage_uris=["data_entry:<uuid>"],
)
```

## Task ops

```python
from e14c.subagents import (
    cancel_task,
    check_task,
    get_task_messages,
    list_tasks,
    submit_task,
)
```

### Parallel submission

Submit all tasks first, then do other work or wait. Check when ready:

```python
lit_id = await submit_task("...", "brca1-study", agent_type="literature")
da_id = await submit_task("...", "brca1-study")
dr_id = await submit_task("...", "brca1-study", agent_type="data-retrieval")
# use wait_for_subagents or perform other tasks, then:
lit_info = await check_task(lit_id)
da_info = await check_task(da_id)
dr_info = await check_task(dr_id)
```

Do not submit more than 10 parallel platform tasks.

### Waiting for results

After submitting tasks, use the `wait_for_subagents` tool. When you wake up,
silently check task status. If nothing completed, wait again. Only speak when
you have substantive results to share. See CHAT.md "What Not to Say" rules.

### List, inspect, and cancel

```python
print(list_tasks(limit=10))
print(list_tasks(agent_type="literature"))
print(list_tasks(project_tag="brca1-study"))

messages = await get_task_messages(task_id)
cancel_task(task_id)
```

## Sub-agent functionality

### Literature

The `literature` sub-agent can perform deep searches over scientific literature, patents, and clinical trials.
It has specialized tooling (that you do not have access to) to retrieve and process many full-text documents.
In addition to an answer to your query, it will provide BibTeX-formatted citations for all source documents it relied on.
Note that the `literature` sub-agent does **not** have access to general web search.
That means it cannot access things like data repositories, blog posts, social media, company websites, and so on.
If your query requires access to such sources, consider using `e14c.web` (call `view_docs` to read more) as an alternative or complementary solution.

### Analysis

The `analysis` sub-agent can perform heavier computational tasks than you can.
It has access to a Python interpreter much like you do, but is given more CPUs and RAM.
Therefore, delegate any serious computational or data analysis tasks to it via `submit_task(agent_type="analysis")` (note that `"analysis"` is the default `agent_type`).
Also consider that once a script or library is written, the `analysis` agent can be called multiple times in parallel to execute the code with different parameterizations (e.g. a hyperparameter sweep).

### Data retrieval

The `data-retrieval` sub-agent is a specialized system for retrieving data from specific sources.
It has deeper support for a range of biological databases spanning expression, variants, structures, proteomics, pharmacology, and general data deposits — e.g. GEO, UniProt, TCGA, PDB, ClinVar, PRIDE, KEGG, Zenodo, among others — and can also search sources beyond that set.
Use this agent when you know either:

1. a specific data source / dataset ID (e.g. GEO accession number)
2. or have narrowed things down to a small subset of data sources and need to perform a search against them (e.g. "find me RNA-seq datasets pertaining to ...")

### Artifact generation

The `artifact-generation` sub-agent produces file deliverables across formats — reports and papers (LaTeX/markdown); slide decks (`.pptx`); Word documents (`.docx`), spreadsheets (`.xlsx`); and infographics, schematics, and diagrams. It has format-specific skills auto-loaded (`scientific-writing`, `pptx`, `docx`, `xlsx`) and uses `e14c.reports.image_generation.generate_image` for non-data figures. Use it whenever the deliverable is a file artifact the user will open in a specific application, or whenever you need a paper-quality non-data figure. Include the target format and any structural requirements in your dispatch query so the sub-agent routes correctly. It can delegate to `literature` for citation verification. Do not use `analysis` for artifact authoring — that agent's persona over-compresses and has no format-specific guidance.

## Dataset search and retrieval

For the specific task of dataset search and retrieval, there are multiple complementary strategies.
Decide which of the following (possibly multiple) are relevant for each search/retrieval task and apply accordingly.

- `e14c.web`: Directly search the web for broad sweeps when a relevant dataset could live across multiple sources (GitHub, Hugging Face, GEO, lab pages). Relatively fast, but may not be as deep as a literature sub-agent. Note that `e14c.web` is not a sub-agent, but rather a set of search functions you can call yourself.
- `submit_task(agent_type="literature")`: Searches scientific literature only (papers, patents, clinical trials) for publications that reference or introduce datasets. Surfaces dataset names and citations, not the data itself. Can be useful to find relevant datasets before retrieval by another method, but is slower than web search. The literature agent does not have access to general web search.
- `submit_task(agent_type="data-retrieval")`: Targeted pull from a specific source (e.g. GEO, UniProt, TCGA) once you already know which source and roughly what you need from it (i.e. a specific dataset ID or targeted search query).

## Available Skills

Use `load_skill` to read a skill's guide before using it. Only load skills relevant to your current task.

- **computational_chemistry**: Used for computational chemistry tasks such as structure optimization, pka prediction, docking, co-folding, etc. via the Rowan API.
- **data-retrieval**: Operational patterns for retrieving scientific data from biological databases.
- **docx**: Use this skill when you need to *edit* or *create* a .docx file. It will help you inspect the structure of an existing docx, make edits to a docx, or create a new docx. Only load this skill if specifically directed to work with a .docx file. If not, prefer to write PDFs as directed by the `scientific-writing` skill.
- **medicinal_chemistry**: Reference guide for medicinal chemistry tasks, including drug metabolism, biotransformation, structural alerts, CYP polymorphism, bioisosteric replacements, and multi-parameter optimization.
- **pptx**: Use this skill you need to *edit* or *create* a .pptx file. It will help you inspect the structure of an existing pptx, make edits to a pptx, or create a new pptx.
- **scientific-writing**: Style, formatting, and citation guidelines for long scientific documents. Auto-loaded by the `artifact-generation` sub-agent; orchestrators should delegate via `submit_task(agent_type="artifact-generation")` rather than loading this skill directly.
- **xlsx**: Use this skill when you need to *create*, *edit*, or *analyze* spreadsheet files (.xlsx, .csv). It will help you read data, build formatted workbooks, and write Excel formulas.

The instrument is a low-cost, open-source multi-powder doser for autonomous additive-manufacturing (AM) alloy and materials discovery. Assume the following capabilities are already in place: a closed-loop gravimetric calibration algorithm with extensive reported calibration data across many powders; an automatic auger / screw auto-change mechanism; and the ability to dispense up to 50 unique powders in one run. The paper will combine mechanical (CAD/3D-printed hardware), electronics/firmware, control software, and a substantial materials-science calibration dataset. For each candidate venue give: full journal name, publisher, scope, approximate impact factor / CiteScore, whether it accepts hardware/methods/dataset papers, open-access options and typical APC, typical time-to-decision, and a 1-3 sentence justification of fit. Explicitly assess Digital Discovery (RSC), HardwareX (Elsevier), Journal of Open Hardware, the Journal of Open Source Hardware/Software, Review of Scientific Instruments (AIP), HardwareX-style instrument venues, Cell Reports Physical Science, Matter, Nature Communications, npj Computational Materials, ACS Central Science, Lab on a Chip, IEEE/ASME Transactions on Mechatronics, Additive Manufacturing, Additive Manufacturing Letters, Materials & Design, Science Advances, PLOS ONE, SoftwareX, and any others you consider strong. Group them into tiers (best-fit/primary, strong secondary, stretch/high-impact, and fallback). Emphasize venues that are genuinely amenable to a hardware-focused, self-driving-lab contribution with calibration data. Cite journal scope pages and representative recent (2020-2025) SDL / open-hardware / automated materials papers published in each.

The optimal venue must genuinely welcome hardware-focused contributions with calibration data, not merely tolerate them as supplementary to a discovery result. The following tiered ranking reflects this analysis, supported by representative recent publications in each journal.

  9→| Tier | Rank | Journal (Full Name) | Publisher | Approx. IF/CiteScore | Accepts Hardware/Methods/Dataset Papers? | OA Options & Typical APC | Typical Time-to-Decision | Fit Justification (1-3 sentences) |

 34→---
 35→
 36→## Tier 1: Best-Fit / Primary Targets
 37→
 38→### 1. Digital Discovery (RSC)
 39→
 40→Digital Discovery is the leading dedicated venue for the intersection of automation, AI, and open-source tools in chemistry and materials science. It explicitly publishes SDL instrument papers, open-hardware designs, and software tools. Critically, it offers a **"Commit"** article type specifically designed for open-source hardware and software updates — as demonstrated by Yoshikawa et al. (2026), who published an open-hardware digital pipette v2 for SDL liquid transfer as a Commit paper, complete with 3D-printed CAD files, Arduino-based firmware, cost analysis, and ISO-standard validation data (yoshikawa2026commitdigitalpipette pages 2-3, yoshikawa2026commitdigitalpipette pages 1-2). The journal has also published a pellet dispensomixer and pellet distributor for automated nanocomposite material compounding as open hardware (Hernández-del-Valle et al., 2024), which directly parallels a multi-powder doser (hernandezdelvalle2024pelletdispensomixerand pages 9-9). Additional closely related work includes autonomous biomimetic solid dispensing (Jiang et al., 2023), the Chemspyd open-source interface for robotic chemistry platforms (Seifrid et al., 2024), and extensive SDL reviews and perspectives including the "frugal twin" concept for low-cost SDLs (Lo et al., 2024) and democratizing SDLs via 3D printing (Doloi et al., 2025) (pelkie2025democratizingselfdrivinglabs pages 16-17, doloi2025democratizingselfdrivinglabs pages 1-2, doloi2025democratizingselfdrivinglabs pages 29-30, maffettoneUnknownyearwhatismissing pages 1-3). The journal's scope encompasses data-driven research, laboratory automation, and open tools for accelerated discovery, making it the single best fit for the described manuscript.
 41→
 42→### 2. HardwareX (Elsevier)
 43→
 44→HardwareX is Elsevier's dedicated open-access journal for open-source scientific hardware. Its structured article template explicitly requires build and operation instructions, validation and characterization data, a bill of materials, and linked repositories — precisely the elements this paper offers (miljkovic2109towardsfairprinciples pages 3-6). The journal enforces open-source licensing across both hardware and software components. HardwareX has published numerous automated laboratory instrument papers, including the OpenWorkstation modular open-source platform for automated workflows (Eggert et al., 2020), open-source microfluidic controllers and sensor platforms (Kehl et al., 2021), syringe pump systems, and various low-cost lab automation tools (wenzel2023openhardwarefrom pages 25-28, eggert2020openworkstationamodular pages 24-25, sarıyer2023openhardwarefor pages 14-15, pearce2020economicsavingsfor pages 2-4). A comprehensive review identified 86 open-source hardware articles from HardwareX spanning instruments using 3D printing and Arduino-based electronics (pearce2020economicsavingsfor pages 2-4). Crucially, the journal charges **no APC**, maximizing accessibility. While its CiteScore (~5.7) is moderate, it delivers maximum reproducibility signaling and community adoption potential. A HardwareX submission would complement a Digital Discovery paper by providing the full reproducibility-focused documentation.
 45→
 46→---
 47→
 48→## Tier 2: Strong Secondary Options
 49→
 50→### 3. Review of Scientific Instruments (AIP)
 51→
 52→RSI is the classic venue for instrument design, fabrication, and qualification papers. It has published directly relevant work including a modular testbed for mechanized powder spreading for AM (Oropeza et al., 2021) and automated electrodeposition systems for self-driving labs (Quinn et al., 2026) (pearce2020economicsavingsfor pages 15-16). RSI values rigorous instrument characterization and calibration data, which aligns well with the extensive gravimetric calibration dataset across many powders. However, RSI does not emphasize open-source licensing or repository-linked reproducibility the way Digital Discovery or HardwareX do, and its impact factor (~1.6) is lower than most other options. It is best suited if the paper is framed primarily as a precision instrumentation contribution rather than an SDL/open-hardware community resource.
 53→
 54→### 4. Additive Manufacturing (Elsevier)
 55→
 56→With an IF of ~11.0, Additive Manufacturing is the highest-impact domain-specific journal for AM research. It would be a strong choice if the manuscript is reframed to emphasize how the doser enables autonomous alloy composition design for AM processes, rather than presenting primarily as a hardware description. High-throughput AM alloy exploration is a very active topic in this journal's community. The fit improves substantially if the paper includes demonstration data showing how the instrument changes alloy exploration throughput or enables compositionally graded powder-bed feedstocks.
 57→
 58→### 5. Additive Manufacturing Letters (Elsevier)
 59→
 60→This newer companion journal offers rapid publication in a shorter letter format. It would suit a concise version of the paper focused on the AM-enabling capability (e.g., 50-powder autonomous dosing for combinatorial alloy design) rather than full open-hardware documentation. Its introductory policies may include reduced or waived APCs.
 61→
 62→### 6. SoftwareX (Elsevier)
 63→
 64→SoftwareX publishes software metapapers with linked code repositories and is free to publish in. It is **not suitable for the main combined paper** but would be an excellent venue for a companion metapaper focused solely on the control/calibration software package, providing a separate citable software DOI. This dual-publication strategy (main hardware paper + SoftwareX software paper) is increasingly common in the SDL community.
 65→
 66→---
 67→
 68→## Tier 3: Stretch / High-Impact Venues
 69→
 70→### 7–9. Nature Communications, Science Advances, Matter
 71→
 72→These journals have published landmark SDL papers: MacLeod et al. (2020, 2022) demonstrated SDLs for thin-film discovery in Science Advances and Pareto-front optimization in Nature Communications; Volk & Abolhasani (2024) published SDL performance metrics in Nature Communications (nematov2025thebrightfuture pages 9-11, nematov2025thebrightfuture pages 11-11); Canty et al. (2025) discussed SDL accessibility in Nature Communications; and Stach et al. (2021) published an autonomous experimentation community perspective in Matter (lookman2026materialsinformaticsemergence pages 37-39). However, **all of these require a major scientific discovery or paradigm-shifting result** enabled by the instrument, not just excellent hardware description and calibration. A purely instrument-focused paper would be returned without review at these venues. They become viable only if the paper includes a closed-loop autonomous alloy discovery campaign yielding significant new materials science results.
 73→
 74→### 10–12. Cell Reports Physical Science, npj Computational Materials, ACS Central Science
 75→
 76→These are progressively less natural fits. Cell Reports Physical Science occasionally publishes platform papers but expects a strong physical-science story. npj Computational Materials is more aligned with computational/data methods than physical hardware (nematov2025thebrightfuture pages 9-11, lookman2026materialsinformaticsemergence pages 37-39). ACS Central Science requires broadly transformative results. All would need the paper to de-emphasize hardware documentation and instead demonstrate paradigm-changing autonomous materials discovery.
 77→
 78→---
 79→
 80→## Tier 4: Domain-Specific & Fallback Options
 81→
 82→### 13. Materials & Design (Elsevier)
 83→
 84→A respectable materials-processing journal (IF ~7.6) that could accommodate the paper if written as a materials-processing/design-enabling contribution with the extensive powder calibration dataset as the primary data offering.
 85→
 86→### 14. Lab on a Chip (RSC)
 87→
 88→Despite being a strong RSC journal, its scope centers on microfluidics and miniaturized lab systems for biology/chemistry (sarıyer2023openhardwarefor pages 14-15). A macro-scale powder doser for AM alloy discovery is significantly off-scope.
 89→
 90→### 15. IEEE/ASME Transactions on Mechatronics
 91→
 92→Good for the mechatronic engineering aspects (auger auto-change mechanism, closed-loop control, firmware) but would require de-emphasizing the materials science calibration dataset and SDL framing. Lengthy review times (~3–6 months) are a drawback.
 93→
 94→### 16. Journal of Open Hardware (Ubiquity Press/Western University)
 95→
 96→Scope is excellent — an open-hardware journal accepting detailed hardware documentation. However, it is an emerging journal with no established impact factor, limited readership, and low visibility in the materials/AM communities (pearce2020economicsavingsfor pages 15-16).
 97→
 98→### 17. PLOS ONE
 99→
100→A reliable fallback that accepts any technically sound paper (IF ~2.9). Open-hardware laboratory instruments have been published in the PLOS ecosystem (pearce2020economicsavingsfor pages 15-16, wenzel2023openhardwarefrom pages 25-28). Useful if other venues reject on novelty grounds, but provides no field-specific prestige.
101→
102→### 18. Journal of Open Source Software (JOSS)
103→
104→Publishes **software only** — no hardware description as the primary content. Excellent for a separate companion software publication for the calibration/control Python package, providing a citable software DOI. Fast review (~2–6 weeks), no APC.
105→
106→### 19. Journal of Open Source Hardware (Community)
107→
108→An emerging venue with very limited publication history and uncertain long-term viability. Not recommended as a primary target.
109→
110→---
111→
112→## Strategic Recommendations
113→
114→**Primary submission:** Digital Discovery (RSC) is the clear top recommendation. Its scope explicitly encompasses open-hardware SDL instruments, it offers the Commit article type for open-source hardware, and it has published the most directly analogous work (pellet dispensomixers, digital pipettes, autonomous solid dispensing) (hernandezdelvalle2024pelletdispensomixerand pages 9-9, yoshikawa2026commitdigitalpipette pages 1-2). The journal also carries a respectable IF (~6.2) and fast turnaround.
115→
116→**Parallel companion submissions:** Consider simultaneously submitting (i) the full hardware documentation and CAD files to HardwareX (free, maximum reproducibility), and (ii) the control/calibration software to JOSS or SoftwareX (free, citable software DOI). This three-paper strategy maximizes visibility, reproducibility, and citability across the open-hardware, SDL, and software communities.
117→
118→**If rejected from Digital Discovery:** Review of Scientific Instruments (for an instrument-focused reframing) or Additive Manufacturing Letters (for a concise AM-application-focused reframing) are strong fallback targets.
119→
120→**If discovery results are available:** If the paper can be expanded to include a substantial autonomous alloy discovery campaign, Nature Communications becomes a realistic stretch target given its track record of publishing SDL work (nematov2025thebrightfuture pages 9-11, nematov2025thebrightfuture pages 11-11).
121→
122→References
123→

Pellet dispensomixer and pellet distributor: open hardware for nanocomposite space exploration via automated material compounding. Digital Discovery, Jul 2024. URL: https://doi.org/10.1039/d4dd00198b, doi:10.1039/d4dd00198b. This article has 1 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.

126→2. Democratizing self-driving labs through user-developed automation infrastructure. ChemRxiv, Feb 2025. URL: https://doi.org/10.26434/chemrxiv-2025-zhkrf, doi:10.26434/chemrxiv-2025-zhkrf. This article has 9 citations.

128→3. (yoshikawa2026commitdigitalpipette pages 1-2): Naruki Yoshikawa, Kevin Angers, Kourosh Darvish, Sargol Okhovatian, Dawn Bannerman, Ilya Yakavets, Milica Radisic, and Alán Aspuru-Guzik. messages.content:
1→Question: Identify and rank the highest-impact peer-reviewed venues (journals and archival conference proceedings) to publish work that leans into the GENERATIVE-CAD aspect of an open-hardware self-driving-laboratory project, in which the mechanical hardware (a multi-powder doser for autonomous AM alloy discovery) is designed via an LLM/AI-driven, code-based generative-CAD pipeline (e.g., CadQuery / build123d / OpenSCAD text-to-CAD, agentic design loops, geometry-kernel verifiers, design-for-additive-manufacturing). Prior literature reviews for this project (the project's own generative-CAD background notes) found that generative-CAD work is published across: ACM Transactions on Graphics (SIGGRAPH / SIGGRAPH Asia), CVPR / ICCV / ECCV and other IEEE/CVF venues, NeurIPS / ICML / ICLR, Computer-Aided Design (Elsevier), Computer-Aided Design and Applications, The International Journal of Advanced Manufacturing Technology, ASME Journal of Mechanical Design, Journal of Computing and Information Science in Engineering (JCISE), Additive Manufacturing, Applied Sciences, Computers in Industry, Advanced Engineering Informatics, and Proceedings of the Design Society. For each candidate venue give: full name, publisher, scope, approximate impact factor / CiteScore / h5-index (for conferences), whether it accepts an applied systems/hardware contribution that uses (rather than purely advances) generative-CAD methods, typical acceptance bar, and a 1-3 sentence justification of fit. Specifically weigh the trade-off that this is an APPLIED hardware/SDL contribution using generative CAD as a design methodology, not a pure ML/graphics methods paper, and flag which high-impact venues would or would not accept it on that basis. Recommend the best high-impact venue(s) that remain reasonably amenable to a hardware-focused, self-driving-lab contribution. Cite venue scope pages and representative recent (2022-2025) generative-CAD papers in each.
  2→
  3→# Venue Analysis for a Generative-CAD / Open-Hardware Self-Driving Laboratory Contribution
  4→
  5→## 1. The Publication Landscape for Generative-CAD Work
  6→
  7→Generative-CAD research—including parametric sequence generation, B-rep synthesis, and LLM-driven text-to-CAD—is published across a broad spectrum of venues. Evidence from recent literature shows that the highest-profile generative-CAD *methods* papers appear in top ML and computer vision venues: CVPR hosted CAD-Llama (Li et al., 2025) for LLM-based parametric CAD generation (li2025cadllamaleveraginglarge pages 8-9), NeurIPS published Text2CAD (Khan et al., 2024) (dong2025histcadgeometricallyconstrained pages 12-12, le2025cadknittercompositionalcad pages 16-17), ICML published SkexGen (Xu et al., 2022) (le2025cadknittercompositionalcad pages 16-17, xu2024brepgenabrep pages 11-12), ICLR has OBJ2CAD-type work under review (houUnknownyearobj2cadobjectorientedtexttocad pages 1-2), and ACM Transactions on Graphics (SIGGRAPH) published BrepGen (Xu et al., 2024) (xu2024brepgenabrep pages 11-12). Applied code-based generative-CAD work using frameworks like CadQuery and OpenSCAD has appeared in engineering journals: Daareyni et al. (2025) published an LLM-to-OpenSCAD pipeline in The International Journal of Advanced Manufacturing Technology (daareyni2025generativeaimeets pages 9-10, daareyni2025generativeaimeets pages 1-2), Schüpbach et al. (2025) published an agentic LLM-CAD framework in Proceedings of the Design Society (gomez2025designautomationtechniques pages 86-89), and the Text-to-CadQuery system (Xie & Ju, 2025) demonstrates CadQuery-specific LLM fine-tuning (xie2505texttocadqueryanew pages 12-15, xie2505texttocadqueryanew pages 10-12).
  8→
  9→Separately, self-driving laboratory (SDL) hardware contributions—particularly open-source, 3D-printed, and modular instruments—are concentrated in Digital Discovery (RSC), which has published open-hardware SDL platforms including the digital pipette (Yoshikawa et al., 2023) and democratized 3D-printed lab automation (Doloi et al., 2025) (doloi2025democratizingselfdrivinglabs pages 29-30, doloi2025democratizingselfdrivinglabs pages 1-2), as well as in HardwareX, which accepts detailed open-source hardware descriptions (lavayssiere2022laboremboxa pages 1-2). Other outlets for SDL hardware include Nature Communications, Chemical Reviews, and domain-specific chemistry/materials journals (pelkie2025democratizingselfdrivinglabs pages 16-17, silva2024shapingthefuture pages 12-14, wenzel2023openhardwarefrom pages 25-28).
 10→
 11→## 2. The Core Trade-Off: Applied Hardware/SDL vs. Pure Methods
 12→
 13→The critical trade-off for this project is that the contribution is primarily an *applied* hardware/SDL system paper—a multi-powder doser for autonomous AM alloy discovery—that *uses* generative-CAD (LLM/AI-driven code-based CAD via CadQuery/build123d/OpenSCAD, with agentic design loops and geometry-kernel verifiers) as a design methodology, rather than advancing generative-CAD methods per se. This distinction fundamentally shapes venue selection:
 14→
 15→**Venues that would NOT accept the paper** (high-impact but methods-focused): ACM TOG/SIGGRAPH, CVPR, ICCV/ECCV, NeurIPS, ICML, and ICLR all publish generative-CAD work, but they require the primary contribution to be a novel ML, vision, or graphics *method*. An applied paper that uses LLM-CAD to design hardware but does not advance the underlying model architecture, training procedure, or representation would almost certainly be desk-rejected at these venues (li2025cadllamaleveraginglarge pages 8-9, xu2024brepgenabrep pages 11-12).
 16→
 17→**Venues that WOULD accept the paper** (applied-systems-friendly): Digital Discovery, IJAMT, ASME JMD/JCISE, Additive Manufacturing, Advanced Engineering Informatics, Computers in Industry, and Proceedings of the Design Society all have track records of publishing applied AI-for-design or hardware/SDL contributions, provided there is sufficient methodological or engineering novelty (doloi2025democratizingselfdrivinglabs pages 1-2, daareyni2025generativeaimeets pages 9-10, gomez2025designautomationtechniques pages 86-89, man2025fromsketchto pages 84-84).
 18→
 19→## 3. Ranked Venue Table
 20→
 21→The following table ranks all candidate venues by overall fit for the described contribution, with impact as a secondary factor.
 22→
 23→| Rank | Venue full name | Publisher | Approximate Impact Factor / CiteScore / h5-index | Scope summary | Accepts applied hardware/SDL contribution? | Acceptance bar | Representative recent generative-CAD paper (2022-2025) | Fit justification |
 24→|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
 25→| 1 | Digital Discovery | Royal Society of Chemistry (RSC) | IF ~6.2; CiteScore ~10 | Publishes digital, automated, AI-enabled chemistry/materials workflows, including self-driving labs, open hardware, and low-cost automation. | Yes | Medium | Yoshikawa et al., “Digital pipette: open hardware for liquid transfer in self-driving laboratories” (2023); Doloi et al., “Democratizing Self-Driving Labs: Advances in Low-Cost 3D Printing for Laboratory Automation” (2025) (doloi2025democratizingselfdrivinglabs pages 1-2, doloi2025democratizingselfdrivinglabs pages 29-30) | Best overall fit if the paper foregrounds the open-hardware SDL contribution and treats generative-CAD as the enabling design methodology. It already publishes SDL hardware, 3D-printed lab automation, and community-facing open systems papers, so a multi-powder doser for autonomous AM alloy discovery is squarely in-scope. |
 26→| 2 | Additive Manufacturing | Elsevier | IF ~11; CiteScore ~19 | Top AM journal covering materials, processes, design-for-AM, AM systems, and integrated design/manufacturing workflows. | Partial | High | AM-driven generative design / DfAM papers are common; e.g., ML-assisted DfAM and topology/generative-design studies cited in the AM literature review stream (Watson 2023 contextual fit via AM-focused generative design) | Highest-impact manufacturing venue still plausibly amenable if the paper emphasizes the doser as novel AM-enabling hardware for alloy discovery and shows that the generative-CAD pipeline materially improved printability, modularity, or DfAM performance. Less ideal if the manuscript is mostly about SDL architecture rather than AM outcomes. |
 27→| 3 | The International Journal of Advanced Manufacturing Technology | Springer Nature | IF ~3.4; CiteScore ~8 | Broad manufacturing journal spanning design, process planning, automation, robotics, CAD/CAM, and applied AI in manufacturing. | Yes | Medium-High | Daareyni et al., “Generative AI meets CAD: enhancing engineering design to manufacturing processes with large language models” (2025); Watson et al., “Generative design of space frames for additive manufacturing technology” (2023) (daareyni2025generativeaimeets pages 9-10, daareyni2025generativeaimeets pages 1-2) | Very strong fit for a paper that integrates LLM-driven code-based CAD with actual fabrication hardware and manufacturing validation. It has direct precedent for OpenSCAD/LLM CAD pipelines and for generative-design-for-AM papers, making it one of the safest journal targets. |
 28→| 4 | Journal of Mechanical Design | ASME | IF ~3.3; CiteScore ~6 | Flagship engineering-design journal focused on methods, theories, and systems for design synthesis, automation, and computational design. | Partial | High | Regenwetter et al., “Deep Generative Models in Engineering Design: A Review” (2022); GenCAD=3D (2025) noted in recent JMD-linked literature (man2025fromsketchto pages 84-84) | Excellent fit if the paper makes a genuine engineering-design-method contribution: e.g., an agentic generative-CAD workflow with verification, parameterized modularity, and DfAM-aware design rules validated on the doser. It is less suitable if the main novelty is the hardware build itself rather than the design methodology. |
 29→| 5 | Journal of Computing and Information Science in Engineering (JCISE) | ASME | IF ~3.1; CiteScore ~6 | Covers computational methods, CAD/CAE/CAM, design automation, geometric computing, and AI/ML for engineering information and systems. | Partial | Medium-High | Recent JCISE/JMD-linked LLM4CAD and CAD-automation literature cluster; code-generation/engineering informatics framing fits the venue’s computational-design remit (man2025fromsketchto pages 84-84) | Strong choice when the core claim is a reusable computational pipeline for text/code-based CAD generation, verification, and design-space exploration, with the hardware as the case study. More amenable than pure ML venues, but still expects a clear computational contribution. |
 30→| 6 | Computer-Aided Design | Elsevier | IF ~3.0; CiteScore ~7 | Core CAD journal for geometric modeling, design automation, computational representations, and CAD-related AI methods. | Partial | High | Simplification of 3D CAD models using GANs (2023, cited as unobtainable in search); broader generative-CAD lineage including B-rep/sequence modeling is central to the venue ecosystem (xie2505texttocadqueryanew pages 12-15, xu2024brepgenabrep pages 11-12) | Strong venue if the manuscript leans into the generative-CAD pipeline itself: code representations, geometry-kernel verification, parametric editability, and CAD automation. Weaker if the paper is mainly an SDL/open-hardware system paper with minimal CAD-method novelty. |
 31→| 7 | Advanced Engineering Informatics | Elsevier | IF ~8.0; CiteScore ~15 | Publishes AI, knowledge-based systems, digital engineering, and informatics for design, construction, and manufacturing. | Yes | High | Generative/AI-for-design and engineering-informatics papers are common; recent agentic/LLM engineering design pipelines are highly consonant with venue norms | Very attractive if the paper stresses the end-to-end digital thread: LLM-driven CAD, validation, knowledge capture, hardware documentation, and closed-loop laboratory deployment. It is more systems-friendly than pure CAD journals, while still rewarding methodological novelty. |
 32→| 8 | Computers in Industry | Elsevier | IF ~10.0; CiteScore ~17 | Focuses on industrially relevant computer-based methods, digitalization, automation, and deployable systems in engineering/manufacturing. | Yes | High | Applied AI/CAD/manufacturing systems papers are typical; recent industrial CAD-generation and automation work fits its translational orientation | Good high-impact option if the doser is presented as a deployable industrial-style digital engineering system rather than a purely academic design-method paper. Better for demonstrable workflow integration and usability than for algorithmic CAD novelty per se. |
 33→| 9 | Proceedings of the Design Society | Cambridge University Press / Design Society | archival proceedings; no standard JIF; CiteScore modest | Archival design-research proceedings covering engineering design methods, tools, and emerging AI-enabled design workflows. | Yes | Medium | Schüpbach et al., “From text to design: a framework to leverage LLM agents for automated CAD generation” (2025) (gomez2025designautomationtechniques pages 86-89) | One of the clearest venues for agentic generative-CAD workflows with a real engineering case study. Lower impact than the best journals, but notably more amenable to an applied paper where the methodological contribution is modest yet meaningful. |
 34→| 10 | Computer-Aided Design and Applications | CAD Solutions / U-turn Press | IF ~0.8; CiteScore ~2 | Application-oriented CAD journal publishing practical CAD methods, implementations, and case studies. | Yes | Medium-Low | Shao & Shao, “Innovation of Apparel Design Based on Deep Generative Modeling and Its Practice of CAD Collaborative Design” (2024) | Plausible outlet for a pragmatic code-based generative-CAD pipeline tied to a hardware case study, especially if the contribution is more implementation-oriented than theoretically deep. Lower prestige, but comparatively forgiving on applied systems work. |
 35→| 11 | HardwareX | Elsevier | IF ~2.5; CiteScore ~4 | Dedicated open-hardware journal for detailed, reproducible scientific hardware descriptions with build files and validation. | Yes | Medium | Laborem Box (2022) and other open-source lab platforms; not generative-CAD-specific, but directly aligned with publishable hardware documentation (lavayssiere2022laboremboxa pages 1-2) | Best venue if the primary goal is to disseminate the open hardware itself with files, BOM, and validation. However, it is not a high-impact generative-CAD venue, so it is better as a companion paper than as the main flagship publication for the CAD/AI angle. |
 36→| 12 | Applied Sciences | MDPI | IF ~2.5; CiteScore ~4 | Broad applied-engineering journal that accepts practical AI/manufacturing/CAD applications across many domains. | Yes | Low-Medium | Yüksel & Börklü, “A generative deep learning approach for improving the mechanical performance of structural components” (2024, cited in search results) | Reasonably amenable to applied system papers and fast-moving AI/manufacturing work, but less selective and less field-defining. Useful fallback if the work is interdisciplinary but does not fit a sharper design/manufacturing venue. |
 37→| 13 | ACM Transactions on Graphics (SIGGRAPH / SIGGRAPH Asia) | ACM | IF ~6.2; h5-index aligned with SIGGRAPH prestige | Premier graphics venue for fundamental advances in geometry, graphics, and 3D generation. | No | Very High | Xu et al., “BrepGen: A B-rep Generative Diffusion Model with Structured Latent Geometry” (2024) (xu2024brepgenabrep pages 11-12) | Extremely high prestige for generative-CAD methods, but a poor fit for an applied SDL hardware paper that merely uses such methods. Acceptance would require a substantial new generative geometry/modeling algorithm, not just a strong hardware deployment. |
 38→| 14 | IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition (CVPR) | IEEE / CVF | h5-index ~389 | Top computer-vision conference publishing novel vision and 3D/CAD generation methods. | No | Very High | Li et al., “CAD-Llama: Leveraging Large Language Models for Computer-Aided Design Parametric 3D Model Generation” (2025) (li2025cadllamaleveraginglarge pages 8-9) | CVPR clearly accepts parametric CAD generation, but only when the core contribution is a new ML/vision method. An applied multi-powder doser paper using generative CAD as a design tool would almost certainly not clear the bar. |
 39→| 15 | International Conference on Learning Representations (ICLR) | OpenReview | h5-index ~286 | Leading ML conference for representation learning and foundation-model methodology. | No | Very High | Seek-CAD / OBJ2CAD-style text-to-CAD work appears in the ICLR orbit or under review, but not as applied hardware papers (houUnknownyearobj2cadobjectorientedtexttocad pages 1-2) | Not suitable unless the paper advances core model architecture, alignment, or training methodology for generative CAD. The applied hardware/SDL framing works against, not for, acceptance. |
 40→| 16 | Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems (NeurIPS) | NeurIPS Foundation | h5-index ~278 | Premier ML venue focused on novel algorithms, theory, and foundational systems for machine learning. | No | Very High | Text2CAD (NeurIPS 2024) as noted in the CAD venue evidence summary (dong2025histcadgeometricallyconstrained pages 12-12, le2025cadknittercompositionalcad pages 16-17) | Like CVPR, NeurIPS is relevant to generative CAD only when the contribution is primarily a new ML method, dataset, or benchmark. An applied hardware paper that uses LLM CAD would not be competitive. |
 41→| 17 | International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML) | ML Research Press | h5-index ~228 | Top ML conference for methodological advances in machine learning. | No | Very High | SkexGen is cited in the ICML-linked generative-CAD literature stream (le2025cadknittercompositionalcad pages 16-17, xu2024brepgenabrep pages 11-12) | Very poor fit for the proposed paper as stated. ICML would only make sense if the generative-CAD pipeline itself introduced a broadly important ML advance. |
 42→| 18 | International Conference on Computer Vision / European Conference on Computer Vision (ICCV / ECCV) | IEEE / CVF / Springer | h5-index ~200+ class | Major vision conferences publishing 3D understanding, reconstruction, and CAD-related learning methods. | No | Very High | DeepCAD and related CAD-sequence modeling appear in the vision-conference ecosystem; CAD-generation has precedent, but as method papers (xie2505texttocadqueryanew pages 12-15, chen2025img2cadconditioned3d pages 9-9) | Similar story to CVPR: the venue family is appropriate for parametric CAD generation research, but not for an applied SDL/hardware manuscript unless the main novelty is a new vision/3D learning method. |
 43→
 44→
 45→*Table: This table ranks peer-reviewed venues for a generative-CAD plus open-hardware self-driving-lab paper, prioritizing fit for an applied hardware/SDL contribution over raw venue prestige. It distinguishes journals and conferences that are realistically receptive from high-impact ML/graphics venues that publish generative CAD but would likely reject an applied systems paper.*
 46→
 47→## 4. Top Recommendations
 48→
 49→Based on the analysis, the recommended publication strategy depends on how the manuscript is framed:
 50→
 51→### Primary Recommendation: Digital Discovery (RSC)
 52→Digital Discovery is the single venue that sits at the intersection of SDL open hardware, AI-driven automation, and accessible 3D-printed instruments for materials discovery. It has published open-hardware SDL platforms (doloi2025democratizingselfdrivinglabs pages 29-30, doloi2025democratizingselfdrivinglabs pages 1-2, maffettoneUnknownyearwhatismissing pages 1-3), has a growing IF (~6.2), and would welcome a paper that foregrounds the multi-powder doser as an AI-designed, open-source SDL module for autonomous AM alloy discovery. The generative-CAD pipeline would serve as the enabling methodology rather than needing to be a standalone ML contribution.
 53→
 54→### High-Impact Alternative: The International Journal of Advanced Manufacturing Technology (Springer)
 55→IJAMT has direct precedent for LLM-driven code-based CAD work applied to manufacturing: Daareyni et al. (2025) published an LLM-to-OpenSCAD pipeline with manufacturing validation in this exact journal (daareyni2025generativeaimeets pages 9-10, daareyni2025generativeaimeets pages 1-2). A paper framing the doser as a case study in LLM-driven design-for-additive-manufacturing with fabrication and functional validation would be highly competitive here.
 56→
 57→### If Emphasizing Design Methodology: ASME Journal of Mechanical Design or JCISE
 58→If the manuscript can make a genuine methodological contribution regarding the agentic design loop, geometry-kernel verification, or DfAM-aware generative-CAD workflow, JMD or JCISE would provide higher prestige within the engineering design community. JMD has published the deep generative models review by Regenwetter et al. (2022) and GenCAD=3D (2025) (man2025fromsketchto pages 84-84), establishing precedent for AI-enabled design synthesis work.
 59→
 60→### If Emphasizing Manufacturing/AM Outcomes: Additive Manufacturing (Elsevier)
 61→With an IF of ~11, Additive Manufacturing is the highest-impact manufacturing-specific venue. However, the paper would need to center on AM-relevant contributions (novel powder dosing mechanisms, multi-material process capabilities, alloy discovery results) rather than the CAD pipeline itself.
 62→
 63→### Complementary Hardware Paper: HardwareX
 64→For maximum reproducibility impact, a companion HardwareX paper providing the full open-hardware documentation (CAD files, BOM, assembly instructions, validation data) would complement a more methodologically focused flagship paper in one of the venues above (lavayssiere2022laboremboxa pages 1-2).
 65→
 66→### Conference Option: Proceedings of the Design Society (ICED/DESIGN Conference)
 67→For rapid dissemination, the Design Society conferences (ICED, DESIGN) accept applied LLM-CAD pipeline papers with engineering case studies, as demonstrated by Schüpbach et al. (2025) (gomez2025designautomationtechniques pages 86-89). This is lower-impact but highly amenable to the described contribution.
 68→
 69→## 5. Venues to Avoid for This Paper
 70→
 71→ACM Transactions on Graphics, CVPR, NeurIPS, ICML, ICLR, and ICCV/ECCV should be avoided unless the manuscript can be reframed around a fundamentally new generative-CAD method (e.g., a novel agentic architecture with broadly applicable improvements over existing text-to-CAD systems, validated on multiple design domains beyond the doser). As currently described—an applied hardware system using generative-CAD as a tool—these venues would reject the paper on scope grounds (li2025cadllamaleveraginglarge pages 8-9, le2025cadknittercompositionalcad pages 16-17, xu2024brepgenabrep pages 11-12, houUnknownyearobj2cadobjectorientedtexttocad pages 1-2).
