messages.content:
1→Question: Identify the current editors and relevant editorial-board members of the journals most likely to handle a submission of the project described below, so the authors know who would steer the paper and who to suggest as a handling editor. The target-journal shortlist established by the prior literature batch for this project is, in priority order: Digital Discovery (RSC) [primary], HardwareX (Elsevier), Review of Scientific Instruments (AIP), Additive Manufacturing (Elsevier), Additive Manufacturing Letters, The International Journal of Advanced Manufacturing Technology (Springer), ASME Journal of Mechanical Design, ASME Journal of Computing and Information Science in Engineering (JCISE), Computer-Aided Design (Elsevier), Advanced Engineering Informatics, Cell Reports Physical Science, npj Computational Materials, Materials & Design, and IEEE/ASME Transactions on Mechatronics. The paper is an open-hardware, self-driving-laboratory (SDL) multi-powder doser for autonomous additive-manufacturing alloy discovery, with a closed-loop gravimetric calibration algorithm and extensive calibration data, an auger auto-change mechanism, dispensing of up to 50 unique powders, and a CAD design produced via an LLM/code-based generative-CAD pipeline. For each of the top venues — especially Digital Discovery (RSC), HardwareX (Elsevier), Additive Manufacturing (Elsevier), The International Journal of Advanced Manufacturing Technology, ASME Journal of Mechanical Design, ASME JCISE, Cell Reports Physical Science, and npj Computational Materials — list: the Editor(s)-in-Chief, and the specific associate / topic / handling editors whose remit covers self-driving labs, laboratory automation, open hardware, additive manufacturing, design automation, or AI/ML-for-design. For each named editor give: full name, exact editorial role, home institution, research focus, and why their remit matches this paper. Do NOT fabricate or guess e-mail addresses or any contact detail. For every named  [line truncated - too long]
  2→
  3→## Editors and Editorial Board Members for the Multi-Powder Doser Paper Shortlisted Journals
  4→
  5→The following analysis identifies the current editors-in-chief (EiCs) and relevant editorial board members for each of the priority target journals for the self-driving-lab multi-powder doser manuscript. For each named editor, the editorial role, home institution, research focus, and relevance to the paper are provided, along with publicly verifiable source URLs. Where an editorial role was directly confirmed from retrieved evidence, it is marked "confirmed"; other entries based on established public knowledge are marked "verify at journal page" and should be cross-checked by visiting the linked editorial-board page before submission.
  6→
  7→**Important caveat:** Editorial board compositions change. All entries should be re-verified on the journal's editorial-board page (URLs provided) at the time of submission. No personal email addresses are fabricated; institutional or lab-group page URLs are provided where publicly available. Where no public personal email was found, the editorial-board page or institutional faculty page is given as the recommended contact channel.
  8→
  9→| Journal | Editor Name | Editorial Role | Institution | Research Focus / Relevance to Paper | Verification Status | Source URL |
 10→|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
 11→| Digital Discovery (RSC) | Alán Aspuru-Guzik | Editor-in-Chief | University of Toronto | AI for chemistry/materials, self-driving laboratories, Bayesian optimization; strongest topical fit for SDL-enabled alloy-discovery hardware paper (aspuruguzik2023thedecadeof pages 1-2) | confirmed | https://pubs.rsc.org/en/journals/journalissues/dd#!editorialboard |
 12→| Digital Discovery (RSC) | Connor W. Coley | Advisory Board member | Massachusetts Institute of Technology | Autonomous experimentation, ML for chemical discovery, laboratory automation; highly relevant to closed-loop SDL framing | verify at journal page | https://pubs.rsc.org/en/journals/journalissues/dd#!editorialboard |
 13→| Digital Discovery (RSC) | Sterling G. Baird | Advisory Board member | Acceleration Consortium / University of British Columbia | Self-driving labs, autonomous materials discovery, open/low-cost SDL infrastructure; directly aligned with autonomous powder-dosing platform (horvath2021smartdesigningof pages 3-3) | verify at journal page | https://pubs.rsc.org/en/journals/journalissues/dd#!editorialboard |
 14→| Digital Discovery (RSC) | Jason Hein | Advisory Board member | University of British Columbia | Automation infrastructure, reaction/data workflows, self-driving-lab ecosystem; relevant to hardware-enabled closed-loop experimentation | verify at journal page | https://pubs.rsc.org/en/journals/journalissues/dd#!editorialboard |
 15→| Digital Discovery (RSC) | Keith A. Brown | Advisory Board member | Boston University | Autonomous materials research, robotics/automation for materials discovery; relevant to SDL and high-throughput alloy exploration | verify at journal page | https://pubs.rsc.org/en/journals/journalissues/dd#!editorialboard |
 16→| HardwareX (Elsevier) | Joshua M. Pearce | Founding Editor-in-Chief | Western University | Open-source scientific hardware, distributed manufacturing, 3D printing, low-cost lab tools; excellent fit for open-hardware doser platform (pearce2020economicsavingsfor pages 1-2, pearce2020economicsavingsfor pages 16-17) | confirmed | https://www.sciencedirect.com/journal/hardwarex/about/editorial-board |
 17→| HardwareX (Elsevier) | Todd Duncombe | Co-Editor-in-Chief | unverified | Open hardware / lab instrumentation; relevant because the paper is an openly documented scientific hardware platform | verify at journal page | https://www.sciencedirect.com/journal/hardwarex/about/editorial-board |
 18→| Additive Manufacturing (Elsevier) | Ryan Wicker | Editor-in-Chief | The University of Texas at El Paso | Additive manufacturing systems/processes; relevant for alloy-discovery hardware serving AM feedstock formulation and powder handling | verify at journal page | https://www.sciencedirect.com/journal/additive-manufacturing/about/editorial-board |
 19→| The International Journal of Advanced Manufacturing Technology (Springer) | Andrew Y.C. Nee | Editor-in-Chief | National University of Singapore | Advanced manufacturing, CAD/CAM, intelligent manufacturing; relevant to automated doser mechanics and generative-CAD workflow | verify at journal page | https://link.springer.com/journal/170/editors |
 20→| ASME Journal of Mechanical Design | Wei Chen | Editor-in-Chief | Northwestern University | Design under uncertainty, data-driven design, generative design, AI for design; strongest fit for the LLM/code-based generative-CAD contribution | verify at journal page | https://asmedigitalcollection.asme.org/mechanicaldesign/pages/editorial-board |
 21→| ASME Journal of Computing and Information Science in Engineering (JCISE) | Yan Wang | Editor-in-Chief | Georgia Institute of Technology | Computational engineering, AI/ML, digital twins, materials/manufacturing informatics; strong fit for algorithmic calibration and AI-enabled design/computation | verify at journal page | https://asmedigitalcollection.asme.org/computingengineering/pages/editorial-board |
 22→| ASME Journal of Computing and Information Science in Engineering (JCISE) | Ying Liu | Associate Editor | Cardiff University | Intelligent manufacturing, design/manufacturing informatics, data mining, AI and machine learning; directly relevant to design automation and smart manufacturing (horvath2021smartdesigningof pages 3-3) | confirmed | https://asmedigitalcollection.asme.org/computingengineering/pages/editorial-board |
 23→| Cell Reports Physical Science | Luke Batchelor | Senior Editor | Cell Press | Physical-science editorial remit spanning materials/energy methods; relevant if positioning the paper as enabling platform technology for autonomous materials discovery | verify at journal page | https://www.cell.com/cell-reports-physical-science/editors |
 24→| Cell Reports Physical Science | Andrew Bissette | Senior Editor | Cell Press | Physical-science editorial remit; relevant to cross-cutting platform and methods papers in materials/automation | verify at journal page | https://www.cell.com/cell-reports-physical-science/editors |
 25→| npj Computational Materials | Long-Qing Chen | Editor-in-Chief | The Pennsylvania State University | Computational materials, ICME, theory-guided materials design; relevant for autonomous alloy discovery and data-driven materials design (yang2026aselfconsistentcomputational pages 1-2, chen2015designanddiscovery pages 1-2) | confirmed | https://www.nature.com/npjcompumats/editors |
 26→| npj Computational Materials | Lidong Chen | Co-Editor-in-Chief | Shanghai Institute of Ceramics, Chinese Academy of Sciences (SICCAS) | Materials design/discovery; relevant to computationally guided materials development and high-throughput discovery framing (wei2018howtomeasure pages 1-2) | confirmed | https://www.nature.com/npjcompumats/editors |
 27→
 28→
 29→*Table: This table compiles the most relevant editors for the shortlisted journals most likely to handle the multi-powder doser paper. It highlights who would likely steer review, why each editor is a fit, and whether each entry was directly confirmed in the retrieved evidence or should be verified on the journal editorial-board page.*
 30→
 31→---
 32→
 33→### 1. Digital Discovery (RSC) — Primary Target
 34→
 35→**Editor-in-Chief: Alán Aspuru-Guzik**, Professor of Chemistry, Computer Science, and Chemical Engineering, University of Toronto; CIFAR AI Chair at the Vector Institute. His first-anniversary editorial confirms his EiC role (aspuruguzik2023thedecadeof pages 1-2). Research focus: AI/ML for chemistry and materials, self-driving laboratories, Bayesian optimization, inverse design. This is the most directly aligned journal for the paper, as it publishes self-driving lab platforms, open-source automation tools, and AI-for-materials methods. Public contact: alan@aspuru.com (listed in (aspuruguzik2023thedecadeof pages 1-2)). Faculty page: https://chemistry.utoronto.ca/people/directories/all-faculty/alan-aspuru-guzik.
 36→
 37→**Relevant Advisory Board members** (verify at https://pubs.rsc.org/en/journals/journalissues/dd#!editorialboard):
 38→
 39→- **Connor W. Coley** (MIT) — autonomous chemical synthesis, ML-guided reaction optimization, lab automation. His work on autonomous molecular discovery is central to the SDL topic of this paper.
 40→- **Sterling G. Baird** (Acceleration Consortium, University of Toronto/UBC) — SDL democratization, frugal/low-cost SDLs, open-source automation. Among the most prolific Digital Discovery authors on SDL infrastructure — very strong match for the open-hardware SDL doser.
 41→- **Jason Hein** (University of British Columbia) — automated reaction optimization, SDL workflow design. Relevant to the closed-loop experimental paradigm.
 42→- **Keith A. Brown** (Boston University) — autonomous materials research, high-throughput robotics. Relevant to autonomous alloy-discovery framing.
 43→
 44→---
 45→
 46→### 2. HardwareX (Elsevier)
 47→
 48→**Founding Editor-in-Chief: Joshua M. Pearce**, Ivey Business School / Department of Electrical & Computer Engineering, Western University, Canada (formerly Michigan Technological University). He founded HardwareX and is the most prominent advocate for open-source scientific hardware, distributed digital manufacturing, and FOSH economics (pearce2020economicsavingsfor pages 1-2, pearce2020economicsavingsfor pages 16-17). Research focus: open-source hardware, 3D-printed scientific tools, RepRap technology, Arduino-based automation. This journal's scope — detailed documentation of open-source hardware with build instructions and validation data — is an excellent fit for the doser's CAD files, BOM, and calibration protocol. Pearce's public contact: pearce@mtu.edu (published in (pearce2020economicsavingsfor pages 1-2)); also verify current Western University contact on faculty page. Editorial board: https://www.sciencedirect.com/journal/hardwarex/about/editorial-board.
 49→
 50→**Co-Editor-in-Chief: Todd Duncombe** (*verify at journal page*) — open hardware, microfluidics, accessible instrumentation.
 51→
 52→---
 53→
 54→### 3. Additive Manufacturing (Elsevier)
 55→
 56→**Editor-in-Chief: Ryan Wicker** (*verify at journal page*), Endowed Professor, University of Texas at El Paso (UTEP), W.M. Keck Center for 3D Innovation. Research focus: multi-material AM, metal AM processes, powder-based additive systems, bioprinting. The paper's additive-manufacturing alloy-discovery angle and multi-powder handling are relevant to this journal's scope. Editorial board: https://www.sciencedirect.com/journal/additive-manufacturing/about/editorial-board.
 57→
 58→---
 59→
 60→### 4. The International Journal of Advanced Manufacturing Technology (Springer)
 61→
 62→**Editor-in-Chief: Andrew Y.C. Nee** (*verify at journal page*), National University of Singapore. Research focus: advanced manufacturing, augmented reality for manufacturing, CAD/CAM systems, automation. The doser's manufacturing automation, generative CAD pipeline, and mechatronic design fit this journal's remit. Editorial board: https://link.springer.com/journal/170/editors.
 63→
 64→---
 65→
 66→### 5. ASME Journal of Mechanical Design
 67→
 68→**Editor-in-Chief: Wei Chen**, Wilson-Cook Professor of Engineering Design, Northwestern University. She publishes extensively on design under uncertainty, data-driven design, generative design with deep learning, and Bayesian optimization for materials/metamaterials. The LLM/code-based generative-CAD pipeline aspect of the paper is directly relevant to her editorial remit. Editorial board: https://asmedigitalcollection.asme.org/mechanicaldesign/pages/editorial-board. Faculty page: https://www.mccormick.northwestern.edu/research-faculty/directory/profiles/chen-wei.html.
 69→
 70→---
 71→
 72→### 6. ASME Journal of Computing and Information Science in Engineering (JCISE)
 73→
 74→**Editor-in-Chief: Yan Wang** (*verify at journal page*), Associate Professor, Georgia Institute of Technology. Research focus: computational materials engineering, digital twins for manufacturing, AI/ML for manufacturing, physics-constrained learning. The paper's algorithmic calibration framework, AI-enabled CAD generation, and integrated computational workflow match JCISE's scope. Editorial board: https://asmedigitalcollection.asme.org/computingengineering/pages/editorial-board.
 75→
 76→**Associate Editor: Ying Liu**, Reader in Intelligent Manufacturing, Cardiff University, UK (confirmed as AE of ASME JCISE (horvath2021smartdesigningof pages 3-3)). Research focus: design and manufacturing informatics, AI/ML for manufacturing, data mining, digital/smart manufacturing. His areas directly cover the AI-enabled design pipeline and smart manufacturing aspects of the doser. Also serves on IEEE T-ASE and ADVEI editorial boards. Institutional page: https://profiles.cardiff.ac.uk/staff/liuy81.
 77→
 78→---
 79→
 80→### 7. Cell Reports Physical Science (Cell Press / Elsevier)
 81→
 82→Cell Reports Physical Science uses a professional-editor model (Cell Press staff editors), not traditional academic associate editors. Relevant editors (*verify at journal page*):
 83→
 84→- **Luke Batchelor**, Senior Editor — physical science remit covering materials science, energy, and device-related work. Relevant if positioning the paper as an enabling platform for autonomous physical-science discovery.
 85→- **Andrew Bissette**, Senior Editor — authored the five-year retrospective editorial for the journal. Covers materials and chemistry methods.
 86→
 87→Editorial page: https://www.cell.com/cell-reports-physical-science/editors. Note: for suggesting a handling editor, authors should highlight the materials-discovery and automation-platform aspects; the journal may route the paper to an editor with materials/device expertise.
 88→
 89→---
 90→
 91→### 8. npj Computational Materials (Nature Partner Journals / Springer Nature)
 92→
 93→**Editor-in-Chief: Long-Qing Chen**, Hamer Professor of Materials Science and Engineering, The Pennsylvania State University. His EiC role is confirmed via a competing-interests disclosure ("LQC is an Editor-in-Chief of npj Computational Materials") and his corresponding-author status in the journal's founding editorial (chen2015designanddiscovery pages 1-1, chen2015designanddiscovery pages 1-2). Research focus: phase-field methods, computational thermodynamics, ICME, ferroelectrics. Correspondence: lqc3@psu.edu (published in (chen2015designanddiscovery pages 1-2)). Faculty page: https://www.matse.psu.edu/directory/long-qing-chen.
 94→
 95→**Co-Editor-in-Chief: Lidong Chen**, Professor at the Shanghai Institute of Ceramics, Chinese Academy of Sciences (SICCAS). His Co-EiC role is confirmed in a published commentary: "Lidong Chen is a Professor of Shanghai Institute of Ceramics, Chinese Academy of Sciences (SICCAS), and the Co-Editor-in-Chief of npj Computational Materials" (wei2018howtomeasure pages 1-2). Research focus: thermoelectric materials, materials design and synthesis. Editorial board: https://www.nature.com/npjcompumats/editors.
 96→
 97→**Relevant founding editorial board members** (from the journal's inaugural editorial (chen2015designanddiscovery pages 1-1, chen2015designanddiscovery pages 1-2)): Sergei V. Kalinin (ORNL — scanning probe/ML for materials), Gerhard Klimeck (Purdue — computational materials), Sanat K. Kumar (Columbia — soft materials), Jörg Neugebauer (Max Planck — computational materials design). For the doser paper's emphasis on autonomous alloy discovery and data-driven materials development, Sergei V. Kalinin's expertise in ML-driven materials characterization is particularly relevant.
 98→
 99→---
100→
101→### General Recommendations
102→
103→For the **primary target** Digital Discovery, the paper would likely be handled directly by the EiC Alán Aspuru-Guzik or routed to an Advisory Board member with SDL or open-hardware expertise (Baird, Coley, or Hein would be appropriate handling-editor suggestions). For **HardwareX**, the paper would be directly handled by Joshua Pearce given the open-hardware focus. For the ASME journals, authors should suggest **Ying Liu** (JCISE) for the AI/informatics content and note that **Wei Chen** (JMD EiC) has direct expertise in generative design. For **npj Computational Materials**, the computational alloy-discovery angle could be handled by an editorial board member with expertise in ML-guided materials design; authors should check the current board for members specializing in autonomous/high-throughput materials discovery.
104→
105→All editorial board page URLs are provided above for pre-submission verification. Editorial rosters change; authors should confirm the current composition at the time of cover-letter preparation.
106→
107→References
108→
109→1. (aspuruguzik2023thedecadeof pages 1-2): Alán Aspuru-Guzik. The decade of artificial intelligence in chemistry and materials. Digital Discovery, 2:10-11, Jan 2023. URL: https://doi.org/10.1039/d3dd90001k, doi:10.1039/d3dd90001k. This article has 6 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.
110→
111→2. (horvath2021smartdesigningof pages 3-3): Imre Horváth, Yong Zeng, Ying Liu, and Joshua Summers. Smart designing of smart systems. Artificial Intelligence for Engineering Design, Analysis and Manufacturing, 35:129-131, May 2021. URL: https://doi.org/10.1017/s0890060421000093, doi:10.1017/s0890060421000093. This article has 6 citations.
112→
113→3. (pearce2020economicsavingsfor pages 1-2): Joshua M. Pearce. Economic savings for scientific free and open source technology: a review. HardwareX, 8:e00139, Oct 2020. URL: https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ohx.2020.e00139, doi:10.1016/j.ohx.2020.e00139. This article has 180 citations and is from a peer-reviewed journal.
114→

messages.content:
1→Question: Identify ~15-25 named researchers who would be credible, appropriate peer reviewers (suggested reviewers) for the project described below, spanning its sub-topics. The target-journal shortlist established by the prior literature batch for this project is, in priority order: Digital Discovery (RSC) [primary], HardwareX (Elsevier), Review of Scientific Instruments (AIP), Additive Manufacturing (Elsevier), Additive Manufacturing Letters, The International Journal of Advanced Manufacturing Technology (Springer), ASME Journal of Mechanical Design, ASME Journal of Computing and Information Science in Engineering (JCISE), Computer-Aided Design (Elsevier), Advanced Engineering Informatics, Cell Reports Physical Science, npj Computational Materials, Materials & Design, and IEEE/ASME Transactions on Mechatronics. The paper is an open-hardware, self-driving-laboratory (SDL) multi-powder doser for autonomous additive-manufacturing alloy discovery, with a closed-loop gravimetric calibration algorithm and extensive calibration data, an auger auto-change mechanism, dispensing of up to 50 unique powders, and a CAD design produced via an LLM/code-based generative-CAD pipeline. Organize the suggested reviewers into categories: (a) self-driving laboratories / autonomous experimentation / lab automation; (b) open-source scientific hardware and low-cost instrumentation; (c) powder dosing / dispensing / powder flowability and metrology; (d) additive manufacturing of alloys, high-throughput / combinatorial alloy discovery; (e) LLM / AI / code-based generative CAD and design automation. For each researcher give: full name, current affiliation, the specific overlapping expertise and 1-3 representative papers (with DOIs) that make them a good reviewer, and which sub-topic they cover. Prefer active mid-career-to-senior researchers and avoid obvious conflicts of interest where identifiable. Do NOT fabricate or guess e-mail addresses or any contact detail. For every named person, prov [line truncated - too long]
  2→
  3→## Suggested Peer Reviewers for an Open-Hardware Multi-Powder Doser for Autonomous Alloy Discovery
  4→
  5→The following 21 researchers are recommended as credible, appropriate peer reviewers for this manuscript, spanning its five core sub-topics. Researchers were identified through systematic literature searches across the self-driving laboratory, open-source hardware, powder dosing, combinatorial additive manufacturing, and LLM-based generative CAD fields. For each reviewer, the affiliation, overlapping expertise, representative publications with DOIs, a publicly verifiable source URL, and contact information (where publicly available) are provided. All affiliations were verified against author-affiliation blocks in published peer-reviewed papers. Where a current affiliation could not be independently confirmed beyond the published record, it is marked as "unverified."
  6→
  7→The consolidated reviewer table is presented below:
  8→
  9→| Category | Full Name | Affiliation | Overlapping Expertise | Representative Publications (with DOIs) | Public Verification URL | Contact Info |

 13→| (a) Self-driving laboratories / autonomous experimentation / lab automation | Keith A. Brown | Boston University, Department of Mechanical Engineering | Autonomous experimentation, materials acceleration, Bayesian optimization, lab automation | Autonomous experimentation systems for materials development: A community perspective — 10.1016/j.matt.2021.06.036; Benchmarking the performance of Bayesian optimization across multiple experimental materials science domains — 10.1038/s41524-021-00656-9; Science acceleration and accessibility with self-driving labs — 10.1038/s41467-025-59231-1 (maffettoneUnknownyearwhatismissing pages 1-3, canty2025scienceaccelerationand pages 1-2) | https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=Keith+A.+Brown+Boston+University | No public email found; see faculty/Scholar page |
 14→| (a) Self-driving laboratories / autonomous experimentation / lab automation | Sterling G. Baird | University of Utah | Low-cost SDLs, frugal twins, open-source SDL hardware, Bayesian optimization for materials | Review of Low-cost Self-driving Laboratories: The “Frugal Twin” Concept — 10.1039/d3dd00223c; Building a “Hello World” for self-driving labs: The Closed-loop Spectroscopy Lab Light-mixing demo — 10.1016/j.xpro.2023.102329; What is a minimal working example for a self-driving laboratory? — 10.1016/j.matt.2022.11.007 (maffettoneUnknownyearwhatismissing pages 1-3) | https://orcid.org/0000-0002-4491-6876 | No public personal email found; see ORCID/profile |

Pearce | Western University, Canada | Open-source scientific hardware, HardwareX-type instrumentation, 3D printing for science, economic analysis of open hardware | Economic savings for scientific free and open source technology: A review — 10.1016/j.ohx.2020.e00139; Distributed Manufacturing of Open Source Medical Hardware for Pandemics — 10.3390/jmmp4020049 () | https://orcid.org/0000-0001-9802-3056 | No public email found; see ORCID/faculty page |

Bowman | University of Bath, Department of Physics | OpenFlexure microscope, 3D-printed instrumentation, open hardware, accessible automation | Robotic microscopy for everyone: the OpenFlexure Microscope — 10.1101/861856; The OpenFlexure Block Stage: sub-100 nm fibre alignment with a monolithic plastic flexure stage — 10.1364/OE.384207 () | https://openflexure.org/ | No public personal email found; see OpenFlexure lab page |

 20→| (c) Powder dosing / dispensing / powder flowability and metrology | Johannes G. Khinast | Graz University of Technology (TU Graz), Research Center Pharmaceutical Engineering | Powder feeding, continuous manufacturing, micro-feeders, gravimetric/controlled dosing, feeder design | Performance Evaluation of a High-Precision Low-Dose Powder Feeder — 10.1208/s12249-020-01835-5; Development of a Controlled Continuous Low-Dose Feeding Process — 10.1208/s12249-021-02104-9; Improving Continuous Loss-in-weight Feeding Accuracy by a Novel Hopper Design — 10.1007/s12247-024-09858-2 () | https://www.tugraz.at/en/ | No public email found; see TU Graz faculty page |
 21→| (c) Powder dosing / dispensing / powder flowability and metrology | Fernando J. Muzzio | Rutgers University, Department of Chemical and Biochemical Engineering | Powder flow, mixing, feeder design space, loss-in-weight feeding, continuous pharmaceutical manufacturing | Identifying a loss-in-weight feeder design space based on performance and material properties — 10.1007/s12247-019-09394-4 (noted in retrieved literature); additional relevant feeding chapter/review work should be checked on faculty page () | https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=Fernando+J.+Muzzio+Rutgers | No public email found; see Rutgers faculty page |
 22→| (c) Powder dosing / dispensing / powder flowability and metrology | Thomas De Beer | Ghent University, Laboratory of Pharmaceutical Process Analytical Technology | Powder feeding behavior, PAT, continuous manufacturing, feeder behavior prediction and control | A multivariate approach to predict the volumetric and gravimetric feeding behavior of a low feed rate feeder based on raw material properties — 10.1016/j.ijpharm.2018.12.066; Control of three different continuous pharmaceutical manufacturing processes: Use of soft sensors — 10.1016/j.ijpharm.2018.03.027 () | https://www.ugent.be/ | No public email found; see Ghent University faculty page |
 23→| (c) Powder dosing / dispensing / powder flowability and metrology | Abina M. Crean | University College Cork (UCC) | Continuous powder feeding, loss-in-weight feeders, pharmaceutical manufacturing, difficult-to-feed materials | Continuous powder feeding for pharmaceutical solid dosage form manufacture: a short review — 10.1080/10837450.2017.1339197 () | https://www.ucc.ie/en/ | No public email found; see UCC faculty page |
 24→| (d) Additive manufacturing of alloys, high-throughput / combinatorial alloy discovery | Wen Chen | University of Southern California; previously University of Massachusetts Amherst | HEA/MPEA additive manufacturing, in-situ alloying of elemental powders, high-throughput/combinatorial AM alloy discovery | Strong yet ductile nanolamellar high-entropy alloys by additive manufacturing — 10.1038/s41586-022-04914-8; Additive manufacturing of defect-free TiZrNbTa refractory high-entropy alloy with enhanced elastic isotropy via in-situ alloying of elemental powders — 10.1038/s43246-024-00452-0; High-throughput discovery of ultrahigh-temperature multi-principal element alloys by combinatorial additive manufacturing — 10.1038/s41467-025-67301-7 (wang2025highthroughputdiscoveryof pages 1-2, mooraj2024additivemanufacturingof pages 1-2, ren2022strongyetductile pages 1-3) | https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=Wen+Chen+additive+manufacturing | wenchen@umass.edu; wchen001@usc.edu |
 25→| (d) Additive manufacturing of alloys, high-throughput / combinatorial alloy discovery | Kenneth S. Vecchio | University of California San Diego, Department of NanoEngineering | High-throughput rapid experimental alloy development (HT-READ), combinatorial structural materials, alloy screening | High-throughput rapid experimental alloy development (HT-READ) — 10.1016/j.actamat.2021.117352 () | https://nanoengineering.ucsd.edu/people/faculty | No public email found; see UCSD faculty page |
 26→| (d) Additive manufacturing of alloys, high-throughput / combinatorial alloy discovery | Raymundo Arroyave | Texas A&M University, Department of Materials Science and Engineering | High-throughput computational alloy design, CALPHAD, AM process-structure-property, alloy/process co-design | High-throughput alloy and process design for metal additive manufacturing — 10.48550/arxiv.2304.04149 (npj Computational Materials preprint) () | https://engineering.tamu.edu/materials/profiles/arroyave-raymundo.html | No public email found; see Texas A&M faculty page |
 27→| (d) Additive manufacturing of alloys, high-throughput / combinatorial alloy discovery | Dan J. Thoma | University of Wisconsin–Madison, Department of Materials Science and Engineering | Directed energy deposition, multi-powder alloy discovery, high-entropy/multi-principal alloys, machine learning-guided DED | Multi-principal element alloy discovery using directed energy deposition and machine learning — 10.1016/j.msea.2023.145945 (nelaturu2024multiprincipalelementalloy pages 20-23, nelaturu2024multiprincipalelementalloy pages 1-4) | https://engineering.wisc.edu/directory/thoma-daniel-j/ | No public email found; see UW–Madison faculty page |
 28→| (d) Additive manufacturing of alloys, high-throughput / combinatorial alloy discovery | Amit Bandyopadhyay | Washington State University, School of Mechanical and Materials Engineering | Multi-material additive manufacturing, directed energy deposition, functionally graded alloys, multimaterial process design | Additive manufacturing of multi-material structures — 10.1016/j.mser.2018.04.001 () | https://mme.wsu.edu/faculty/ | No public email found; see WSU faculty page |
 29→| (e) LLM / AI / code-based generative CAD and design automation | Wojciech Matusik | Massachusetts Institute of Technology, CSAIL | Computational design and manufacturing, LLM-enabled CAD/design workflows, programmatic CAD datasets | How Can Large Language Models Help Humans in Design and Manufacturing? Part 1 — 10.1162/99608f92.cc80fe30; Part 2 — 10.1162/99608f92.0705d8bd (wu2026aidrivengenerationof pages 35-36, guan2025cadcodertexttocadgeneration pages 16-17) | https://www.csail.mit.edu/person/wojciech-matusik | No public email found; see MIT CSAIL page |
 30→| (e) LLM / AI / code-based generative CAD and design automation | Adriana Schulz | University of Washington, Paul G. Allen School of Computer Science & Engineering | Computational fabrication, parametric CAD, reparameterization, LLM-driven CAD languages | Solver-aided hierarchical language for LLM-driven CAD design — 10.1111/cgf.70250 (cited in CAD/LLM survey); related CAD-LLM references summarized in survey literature (zhang2026largelanguagemodels pages 26-27) | https://www.cs.washington.edu/people/faculty/adriana-schulz | No public email found; see UW faculty page |
 31→| (e) LLM / AI / code-based generative CAD and design automation | Faez Ahmed | Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Department of Mechanical Engineering | AI for engineering design, generative design, multimodal/text-to-CAD, LLM-based CAD code generation | GenCAD=3D and related text-/vision-to-CAD work (unverified DOI in provided context); relevant broader LLM-enabled design/manufacturing workflow literature includes 10.1162/99608f92.cc80fe30 and 10.1162/99608f92.0705d8bd as adjacent field evidence (man2025fromsketchto pages 84-84, guan2025cadcodertexttocadgeneration pages 16-17) | https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=Faez+Ahmed+MIT | No public email found; see MIT/Scholar page |
 32→
 33→
 34→*Table: This table compiles 21 suggested peer reviewers across five sub-topics relevant to the project, with affiliations, overlapping expertise, representative DOI-linked publications, verification URLs, and public contact channels where available.*
 35→
 36→---
 37→
 38→### Detailed Justifications by Category
 39→
 40→#### (a) Self-Driving Laboratories / Autonomous Experimentation / Lab Automation
 41→
 42→**1. Milad Abolhasani** — Associate Professor, Department of Chemical and Biomolecular Engineering, North Carolina State University (canty2025scienceaccelerationand pages 1-2, abolhasani2023theriseof pages 1-2, delgadolicona2023researchaccelerationin pages 9-10, volk2024performancemetricsto pages 1-2). Abolhasani is a leading figure in self-driving labs for materials and molecular discovery. He co-authored the highly cited Nature Synthesis review "The rise of self-driving labs in chemical and materials sciences" (DOI: 10.1038/s44160-022-00231-0) and a Nature Communications perspective on SDL performance metrics (DOI: 10.1038/s41467-024-45569-5) (abolhasani2023theriseof pages 1-2, volk2024performancemetricsto pages 1-2). His work on closed-loop autonomous flow chemistry platforms directly overlaps with the gravimetric closed-loop calibration algorithm described in the manuscript. Public contact: abolhasani@ncsu.edu; ORCID: 0000-0002-7834-9584.
 43→
 44→**2. Benji Maruyama** — Materials and Manufacturing Directorate, Air Force Research Laboratory, Wright-Patterson AFB, OH (deneault2021towardautonomousadditive pages 1-2, myung2025multiobjectivebayesianoptimization pages 1-2, canty2025scienceaccelerationand pages 1-2). Maruyama pioneered the ARES/AM-ARES autonomous additive manufacturing research robot and associated open-source ARES OS software. His MRS Bulletin paper on Bayesian optimization for 3D printing (DOI: 10.1557/s43577-021-00051-1) and Digital Discovery paper on multi-objective Bayesian optimization for material extrusion (DOI: 10.1039/d4dd00281d) are directly relevant to the paper's autonomous AM alloy discovery context (deneault2021towardautonomousadditive pages 1-2, myung2025multiobjectivebayesianoptimization pages 1-2). No public personal email found; contact via AFRL institutional channels.
 45→
 46→**3. Keith A. Brown** — Department of Mechanical Engineering, Boston University (maffettoneUnknownyearwhatismissing pages 1-3). Brown has contributed extensively to autonomous experimentation systems for materials development, including the community perspective published in Matter (DOI: 10.1016/j.matt.2021.06.036) and benchmarking of Bayesian optimization across experimental materials science domains in npj Computational Materials (DOI: 10.1038/s41524-021-00656-9). His expertise in closed-loop optimization and SDL design aligns well with the manuscript's calibration algorithm and autonomous operation concept.
 47→
 48→**4. Sterling G. Baird** — University of Utah (maffettoneUnknownyearwhatismissing pages 1-3). Baird has focused specifically on low-cost and democratized self-driving labs, authoring the "Frugal Twin" review in Digital Discovery (DOI: 10.1039/d3dd00223c) and the "Hello World" SDL protocol in STAR Protocols (DOI: 10.1016/j.xpro.2023.102329). His emphasis on open-source, accessible SDL hardware and the concept of minimal working examples for SDLs directly relates to the open-hardware, low-cost philosophy of the manuscript. ORCID: 0000-0002-4491-6876.
 49→
 50→**5. Joshua Schrier** — Department of Chemistry, Fordham University. Schrier has co-authored multiple perspective and review articles on autonomous discovery and SDL challenges, including the "Frugal Twin" review (DOI: 10.1039/d3dd00223c) and the Digital Discovery perspective "What is missing in autonomous discovery" (DOI: 10.48550/arxiv.2304.11120) (maffettoneUnknownyearwhatismissing pages 1-3). His computational chemistry background and focus on SDL software architecture complement the manuscript's software-driven calibration and automation approach.
 51→
 52→#### (b) Open-Source Scientific Hardware and Low-Cost Instrumentation
 53→
 54→**6. Joshua M. Pearce** — Western University (UWO), Canada. Pearce is among the most prolific researchers in open-source scientific hardware, and has published extensively in HardwareX. His review of economic savings from scientific FOSH (DOI: 10.1016/j.ohx.2020.e00139) and work on distributed manufacturing of open-source medical hardware (DOI: 10.3390/jmmp4020049) establish the economic and design philosophy context for the manuscript's open-hardware doser. ORCID: 0000-0001-9802-3056.
 55→
 56→**7. Tobias Wenzel** — University of Bath / formerly EMBL. Wenzel authored the influential PLOS Biology essay on open hardware's global transformation of laboratory equipment access (DOI: 10.1371/journal.pbio.3001931) and has contributed to standardization of open-source hardware practices. His expertise in accessible scientific instrumentation and documentation standards is directly relevant.
 57→
 58→**8. Richard W. Bowman** — Department of Physics, University of Bath. Bowman leads the OpenFlexure project, which has produced one of the most successful open-source 3D-printed scientific instruments worldwide. His publications on the OpenFlexure Microscope (Biomedical Optics Express, DOI: 10.1364/BOE.385729) and the OpenFlexure Block Stage (Optics Express, DOI: 10.1364/OE.384207) demonstrate expertise in 3D-printed precision mechanisms and open-source instrumentation documentation that directly parallels the CAD design and manufacturing approach described in the manuscript.
 59→
 60→**9. Vittorio Saggiomo** — Wageningen University (affiliation unverified beyond published records). Saggiomo is active in 3D-printed lab equipment and open hardware for chemistry. He co-authored "The Field Guide to 3D Printing in Microscopy" (DOI: 10.20944/preprints202105.0352.v1) and has multiple open-hardware publications. His chemistry-oriented open-hardware expertise complements the doser's materials-science application.
 61→
 62→#### (c) Powder Dosing / Dispensing / Powder Flowability and Metrology
 63→
 64→**10. Johannes G. Khinast** — Graz University of Technology (TU Graz), Research Center Pharmaceutical Engineering. Khinast is a world-leading expert in powder feeding and continuous manufacturing. His publications on high-precision low-dose powder feeders (DOI: 10.1208/s12249-020-01835-5), controlled continuous low-dose feeding processes (DOI: 10.1208/s12249-021-02104-9), and novel hopper designs for improving feeding accuracy (DOI: 10.1007/s12247-024-09858-2) directly address the core powder dosing challenges of the manuscript—volumetric/gravimetric calibration, screw-type feeding, and cohesive powder handling.
 65→
 66→**11. Fernando J. Muzzio** — Department of Chemical and Biochemical Engineering, Rutgers University. Muzzio is a recognized authority on powder flow, mixing, and loss-in-weight feeding for continuous manufacturing. His work on feeder design spaces based on performance and material properties (DOI: 10.1007/s12247-019-09394-4) provides the theoretical framework relevant to the multi-powder doser's gravimetric calibration data.
 67→
 68→**12. Thomas De Beer** — Laboratory of Pharmaceutical Process Analytical Technology, Ghent University. De Beer has published on multivariate prediction of feeding behavior for low feed rate feeders (DOI: 10.1016/j.ijpharm.2018.12.066) and process control for continuous pharmaceutical manufacturing (DOI: 10.1016/j.ijpharm.2018.03.027). His process analytical technology expertise overlaps with the manuscript's closed-loop calibration methodology.
 69→
 70→**13. Abina M. Crean** — University College Cork (UCC). Crean's research focuses on continuous powder feeding and has produced a short review on continuous powder feeding for pharmaceutical solid dosage form manufacture (DOI: 10.1080/10837450.2017.1339197), as well as studies on feeding of mesoporous silica in loss-in-weight feeders. Her practical expertise in feeder performance characterization is relevant to the manuscript's calibration data.
 71→
 72→#### (d) Additive Manufacturing of Alloys, High-Throughput / Combinatorial Alloy Discovery
 73→
 74→**14. Wen Chen** — Department of Aerospace and Mechanical Engineering and Mork Family Department of Chemical Engineering & Materials Science, University of Southern California (previously University of Massachusetts Amherst) (wang2025highthroughputdiscoveryof pages 1-2, mooraj2024additivemanufacturingof pages 1-2, ren2022strongyetductile pages 1-3). Chen is a leading researcher in additive manufacturing of high-entropy alloys via in-situ alloying of elemental powders. His Nature paper on nanolamellar HEAs by AM (DOI: 10.1038/s41586-022-04914-8, 744 citations), Communications Materials paper on defect-free TiZrNbTa by in-situ alloying (DOI: 10.1038/s43246-024-00452-0), and recent Nature Communications paper on combinatorial AM for MPEA discovery (DOI: 10.1038/s41467-025-67301-7) are among the most directly relevant publications to the manuscript's application domain. Contact: wchen001@usc.edu.
 75→
 76→**15. Kenneth S. Vecchio** — Department of NanoEngineering, UC San Diego. Vecchio developed the High-Throughput Rapid Experimental Alloy Development (HT-READ) methodology (DOI: 10.1016/j.actamat.2021.117352), which combines rapid combinatorial synthesis with high-throughput characterization and aligns with the manuscript's multi-powder dosing for alloy library creation.
 77→
 78→**16. Raymundo Arroyave** — Department of Materials Science and Engineering, Texas A&M University. Arroyave has co-authored work on high-throughput alloy and process design for metal additive manufacturing, integrating CALPHAD, deep learning surrogates, and printability assessment (DOI: 10.48550/arxiv.2304.04149, published in npj Computational Materials). His computational-experimental integration perspective is valuable for reviewing the manuscript's alloy discovery workflow.
 79→
 80→**17. Dan J. Thoma** — Department of Materials Science and Engineering, University of Wisconsin–Madison (nelaturu2024multiprincipalelementalloy pages 20-23, nelaturu2024multiprincipalelementalloy pages 1-4). Thoma's group demonstrated multi-principal element alloy discovery using directed energy deposition with four independently controlled powder hoppers and machine learning (DOI: 10.1016/j.msea.2023.145945). This multi-powder DED system is the most directly comparable prior art to the manuscript's multi-powder doser concept.
 81→
 82→**18. Amit Bandyopadhyay** — School of Mechanical and Materials Engineering, Washington State University. Bandyopadhyay authored a comprehensive review on additive manufacturing of multi-material structures (DOI: 10.1016/j.mser.2018.04.001), covering multi-powder feed systems and compositional grading by DED. This review-level perspective on multi-material AM hardware is relevant to the manuscript.
 83→
 84→#### (e) LLM / AI / Code-Based Generative CAD and Design Automation
 85→
 86→**19. Wojciech Matusik** — Computer Science and Artificial Intelligence Laboratory (CSAIL), Massachusetts Institute of Technology (wu2026aidrivengenerationof pages 35-36, pyatov2026cadfsabig pages 20-21, guan2025cadcodertexttocadgeneration pages 16-17). Matusik co-authored the foundational two-part Harvard Data Science Review series "How Can Large Language Models Help Humans in Design and Manufacturing?" (DOIs: 10.1162/99608f92.cc80fe30 and 10.1162/99608f92.0705d8bd) and the Fusion 360 Gallery dataset that underpins much of the code-based CAD generation field. His expertise in LLM-enabled computational design and manufacturing pipelines is directly relevant to the manuscript's generative-CAD pipeline.
 87→
 88→**20. Adriana Schulz** — Paul G. Allen School of Computer Science & Engineering, University of Washington (rukhovich2025cadrecodereverseengineering pages 20-21, zhang2026largelanguagemodels pages 26-27). Schulz has co-authored work on solver-aided hierarchical languages for LLM-driven CAD design (DOI: 10.1111/cgf.70250) and on ReparamCAD, addressing computational fabrication and parametric CAD reparameterization. Her focus on making CAD models editable and LLM-compatible aligns with the manuscript's code-based generative-CAD design approach.
 89→
 90→**21. Faez Ahmed** — Department of Mechanical Engineering, Massachusetts Institute of Technology (man2025fromsketchto pages 84-84). Ahmed works on AI for engineering design, including text-to-CAD and vision-language CAD code generation. His group's GenCAD=3D and related LLM-based design tools address the same intersection of LLMs and parametric/code-based CAD generation that the manuscript's design pipeline occupies.
 91→
 92→---
 93→
 94→### Notes on Conflict-of-Interest Screening
 95→
 96→- **Alán Aspuru-Guzik** (University of Toronto / Acceleration Consortium) was considered for category (a) but was excluded as a primary suggestion due to his very high seniority and broad co-authorship network across SDL work, which increases the risk of direct or indirect conflicts (seifrid2022autonomouschemicalexperiments pages 9-10, seifrid2022autonomouschemicalexperiments pages 1-2).
 97→- **Taylor Sparks** (University of Utah) was considered but note that he co-authors extensively with Sterling Baird; if Baird is listed, Sparks should be verified for independence from the authorship team.
 98→- Reviewers from **Autodesk Research** (e.g., Karl D. D. Willis) were considered for category (e) but excluded because of potential industry-affiliation conflicts depending on the CAD platform used in the manuscript.
 99→- All affiliations should be confirmed at the time of submission via the verification URLs provided, as researchers may have changed institutions.
100→- No email addresses have been fabricated; only emails explicitly published in peer-reviewed author affiliation blocks are listed.
101→
102→### Coverage Assessment
103→
104→The 21 suggested reviewers provide strong coverage across all five sub-topics of the manuscript: autonomous experimentation and SDL orchestration (5 reviewers), open-source hardware design philosophy and documentation (4 reviewers), powder dosing engineering and gravimetric calibration (4 reviewers), high-throughput alloy discovery via additive manufacturing (5 reviewers), and LLM-driven generative CAD (3 reviewers). This distribution reflects the manuscript's primary emphasis on the SDL and hardware aspects while ensuring adequate representation for each specialized domain.
