messages.content:
1→Question: Identify and rank the best conferences, workshops, and symposia at which to present the project described below, covering both the self-driving-lab / materials angle and the generative-CAD angle. The target-journal shortlist established by the prior literature batch for this project is, in priority order: Digital Discovery (RSC) [primary], HardwareX (Elsevier), Review of Scientific Instruments (AIP), Additive Manufacturing (Elsevier), Additive Manufacturing Letters, The International Journal of Advanced Manufacturing Technology (Springer), ASME Journal of Mechanical Design, ASME Journal of Computing and Information Science in Engineering (JCISE), Computer-Aided Design (Elsevier), Advanced Engineering Informatics, Cell Reports Physical Science, npj Computational Materials, Materials & Design, and IEEE/ASME Transactions on Mechatronics. The paper is an open-hardware, self-driving-laboratory (SDL) multi-powder doser for autonomous additive-manufacturing alloy discovery, with a closed-loop gravimetric calibration algorithm and extensive calibration data, an auger auto-change mechanism, dispensing of up to 50 unique powders, and a CAD design produced via an LLM/code-based generative-CAD pipeline. Assess specifically: the Acceleration Consortium / self-driving-lab meetings and AI-for-materials acceleration workshops; MRS (Materials Research Society) Spring and Fall Meetings and relevant symposia; TMS Annual Meeting; the AIChE Annual Meeting; Faraday Discussions and RSC automation/AI meetings; Gordon Research Conferences relevant to autonomous experimentation or AM; additive-manufacturing conferences (RAPID + TCT, ASTM ICAM, Solid Freeform Fabrication Symposium); design-automation venues (ASME IDETC/CIE — Design Automation Conference and CIE); open-hardware venues (GOSH — Gathering for Open Science Hardware); and ML/vision/graphics venues and their workshops relevant to generative CAD (NeurIPS, ICML, ICLR AI4Science / AI4Mat / ML4Materials workshops, CVPR, SIGGRA [line truncated - too long]
  2→
  3→# Conference, Workshop, and Symposium Recommendations for the Open-Hardware SDL Multi-Powder Doser with Generative-CAD Pipeline
  4→
  5→## Overview
  6→
  7→The project—an open-hardware, self-driving-laboratory (SDL) multi-powder doser for autonomous additive-manufacturing (AM) alloy discovery, featuring closed-loop gravimetric calibration, an auger auto-change mechanism, 50-powder capacity, and an LLM/code-based generative-CAD pipeline—sits at the intersection of several communities: (1) self-driving-lab / autonomous experimentation, (2) materials science and metallurgy, (3) additive manufacturing, (4) engineering design automation and generative CAD, and (5) open science hardware. Below, venues are assessed, tier-ranked, and annotated with detailed metadata. Venues are grouped into **Best-Fit**, **Strong**, and **Stretch** tiers based on their alignment with a hardware-focused SDL contribution.
  8→
  9→The full tier-ranked summary of all 20 assessed venues is presented in the table below:
 10→
 11→| Tier | Venue Full Name | Organizer | Scope | Typical Timing/Location | Submission Format | Selectivity/Prestige | Fit Justification |
 12→|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
 13→| Best-Fit | Accelerate Conference | Acceleration Consortium, University of Toronto | Self-driving labs, materials acceleration platforms, autonomous experimentation, community demos/workshops | Annual; recent editions in Toronto/Vancouver; often summer-fall; exact future location/year can vary | Typically abstract/poster/demo/workshop-style participation; usually non-archival or lightly archival; workshop/demonstration friendly | High relevance inside SDL community; prestige is field-specific rather than society-wide | Probably the single best audience for an open-hardware SDL doser because the community already values low-cost, user-developed automation infrastructure, calibration workflows, and democratized hardware. It is also unusually receptive to system demonstrations and practical infrastructure rather than only algorithmic novelty. (pelkie2025democratizingselfdrivinglabs pages 1-3, miret2026ai4maticlr2026iclr2026 pages 4-6) |
 14→| Best-Fit | Faraday Discussions (especially data-driven discovery / AI-for-chemistry-materials topics) | Royal Society of Chemistry (RSC) | High-level themed meetings on chemistry/materials topics; for this project, data-driven discovery, automation, AI, and self-driving-lab adjacent themes are most relevant | Topic-dependent; held in the UK and internationally; cadence depends on theme rather than one fixed annual slot | Distinctive pre-submitted paper plus oral discussion model; archival in *Faraday Discussions* with discussion record published | Very prestigious, small, curated, discussion-intensive | Best if the manuscript is framed around closed-loop gravimetric calibration, autonomous materials-discovery infrastructure, and lessons for data-driven experimentation. Less ideal for purely mechanical build details, but excellent for a polished SDL systems paper aligned with *Digital Discovery* and RSC audiences. (cooper2025concludingremarksfaraday pages 1-3, cooper2025concludingremarksfaraday pages 16-18, cooper2025concludingremarksfaraday pages 23-26) |
 15→| Best-Fit | Materials Research Society (MRS) Fall Meeting | Materials Research Society | Broad materials meeting with recurring symposia on materials informatics, autonomous experimentation, AI for materials, and advanced manufacturing | Annual; typically late Nov/Dec in Boston, MA | Usually abstract-based talks/posters; archival proceedings uncommon for most symposia | Very high prestige in materials science; broad and visible | Strong fit for the alloy-discovery and SDL materials angle, especially if submitted to a symposium on autonomous experimentation, materials acceleration, or manufacturing/data infrastructure. Hardware-heavy AM dosing can stand out if coupled to compelling calibration data and a clear autonomous-discovery use case. (silva2024shapingthefuture pages 12-14, low2025selfdrivinglaboratoriestranslating pages 7-7) |
 16→| Best-Fit | Solid Freeform Fabrication Symposium (SFF Symposium) – An Additive Manufacturing Conference | The University of Texas at Austin | Deeply technical additive manufacturing conference spanning processes, feedstocks, design, materials, and machine development | Annual; typically August; Austin, Texas | Full paper in proceedings; archival/openly accessible conference papers are a norm for many contributions | High prestige in AM research, especially for technical depth and engineering rigor | Excellent venue for the AM hardware side: powder handling, dosing precision, alloy-feedstock preparation, and integration into AM workflows. It is better than most AM venues for a detailed engineering paper because the audience accepts apparatus, methods, and calibration-rich contributions. (sperry2023formationofeasytoremove pages 15-15) |
 17→| Best-Fit | Shaping the Future of Self-Driving Autonomous Laboratories Workshop | Oak Ridge National Laboratory (ORNL) / DOE-lab ecosystem | National-lab-focused workshop on SDL architectures, instrumentation integration, AI/ML decision-making, data standards, workforce, and ecosystem building | Recent documented edition: Nov. 7–8, 2024, Denver, CO; future continuity is plausible but specific series recurrence is unverified | Workshop participation via talks/panels/breakouts; non-archival meeting format documented in technical report | High strategic relevance; prestige is networking/roadmapping rather than publication-driven | Outstanding fit if the goal is feedback, collaborators, and visibility among national-lab and infrastructure builders. The project’s open hardware, multi-instrument integration, and calibration/control aspects align especially well with the workshop’s instrumentation, interoperability, and standards focus. (silva2024shapingthefuture pages 1-4, silva2024shapingthefuture pages 16-17, silva2024shapingthefuture pages 4-6) |
 18→| Strong | The Minerals, Metals & Materials Society (TMS) Annual Meeting & Exhibition | TMS | Metals, alloys, ICME, process metallurgy, manufacturing, and data-enabled materials engineering | Annual; typically February-March; rotating North American cities | Usually abstract-based presentations; some tracks lead to proceedings or journal special issues | High prestige in metallurgy and structural materials | Very strong for the alloy-discovery and metallurgy audience, especially if the doser is positioned as enabling compositional exploration for powder-based AM alloys. Less naturally open-hardware-focused than SDL meetings, but stronger on alloy relevance than many automation venues. (silva2024shapingthefuture pages 12-14, nematov2025thebrightfuture pages 11-11) |
 19→| Strong | Materials Research Society (MRS) Spring Meeting | Materials Research Society | Broad materials conference with symposia on informatics, automation, energy materials, and emerging fabrication methods | Annual; typically April; often Phoenix, AZ or Seattle, WA | Usually abstract-only talk/poster submissions; mostly non-archival | Very high prestige in materials science | Similar benefits to MRS Fall, though often a bit less central for some physical-metallurgy communities. A good option if there is a spring symposium specifically on autonomous experimentation, materials acceleration, or digital manufacturing. (silva2024shapingthefuture pages 12-14, low2025selfdrivinglaboratoriestranslating pages 7-7) |
 20→| Strong | ASME International Design Engineering Technical Conferences & Computers and Information in Engineering Conference (IDETC/CIE), especially DAC and CIE tracks | American Society of Mechanical Engineers (ASME) | Engineering design, design automation, computational design, CAD/CAM, engineering informatics, AI-enabled design methods | Annual; typically August; recent documented edition Aug. 17–20, 2025 in Anaheim, CA | Full papers in ASME conference proceedings; archival | Strong prestige in engineering design and design automation | Best venue for the generative-CAD half if the story emphasizes the LLM/code-based CAD pipeline, parametric design generation, and reproducible hardware design automation. It is not the strongest SDL venue, but it is the best serious archival design-engineering venue on your list for the CAD contribution. (seng2025detc2025168839 pages 1-2) |
 21→| Strong | AIChE Annual Meeting | American Institute of Chemical Engineers (AIChE) | Chemical engineering, process systems, laboratory automation, robotics, optimization, digitalization, autonomous experimentation | Annual; typically November; rotating U.S. cities | Usually abstract-based conference talks; some invited or extended submissions; mostly non-archival | High prestige in chemical/process engineering | Good fit if the work is framed as a dosing/dispensing platform, process-control problem, or autonomous experimentation module rather than primarily as AM hardware. Hardware rigor and closed-loop calibration can resonate, but AM-alloy relevance may be less central than at TMS/SFF. (cooper2025concludingremarksfaraday pages 1-3, low2025selfdrivinglaboratoriestranslating pages 7-7) |
 22→| Strong | AI4Mat Workshop at NeurIPS | Workshop organizers under the NeurIPS workshop program; AI4Mat community | AI for accelerated materials design, materials discovery, inverse design, benchmarks, tools, and findings | At NeurIPS in December; location follows NeurIPS host city | Non-archival workshop papers via OpenReview; categories have included papers, tools & findings, tiny papers, and themed tracks | High visibility workshop attached to elite ML venue; workshop prestige is strong but below main conference | Good fit only if the paper foregrounds the autonomous materials-discovery framing, data/benchmark value, or the generative-CAD/AI pipeline as a methodological contribution. Hardware-heavy apparatus papers are less central here, but the workshop is notably friendlier to tools and findings than most ML venues. (miret2026ai4maticlr2026iclr2026 pages 4-6, miret2026ai4maticlr2026iclr2026 pages 1-2, miret2026ai4maticlr2026iclr2026 pages 2-4) |
 23→| Strong | AI4Mat Workshop at ICLR | Workshop organizers under the ICLR workshop program; AI4Mat community | AI for accelerated materials design with emphasis on ML methods, data, tools, and interdisciplinary materials applications | At ICLR in spring; location follows ICLR host city | Non-archival workshop papers, tiny papers, findings/tools tracks, OpenReview-based review | High visibility workshop; somewhat more methods-focused than hardware-focused | Similar to AI4Mat-NeurIPS, but potentially slightly easier to place interdisciplinary tool papers. Best if you stress the generative-CAD pipeline or autonomous design loop rather than only the electromechanical doser build. (miret2026ai4maticlr2026iclr2026 pages 4-6, miret2026ai4maticlr2026iclr2026 pages 1-2, miret2026ai4maticlr2026iclr2026 pages 2-4) |
 24→| Strong | Gathering for Open Science Hardware (GOSH) | Global Open Science Hardware community | Open-source/open-science hardware, documentation, accessibility, community building, inclusive hardware practices | Irregular but recurring global gatherings; location varies; participation model is community-driven | Unconference/workshops/sessions rather than formal archival papers; open participation and hands-on sessions | High reputation within open-hardware community; low conventional academic selectivity | Excellent for the open-hardware dissemination mission, documentation feedback, and community adoption. It is weaker as a conventional academic publication venue, but few places are better for finding users, contributors, and best practices for sharing the doser design responsibly and reproducibly. (arancio2021openingupthe pages 15-17, arancio2021openingupthe pages 20-23, arancio2021openingupthe pages 10-12) |
 25→| Strong | ASTM International Conference on Additive Manufacturing (ICAM) | ASTM International, often with AM Center of Excellence partners | Additive manufacturing processes, feedstocks, qualification, standards, industrial adoption, materials and testing | Annual; timing/location vary internationally | Usually abstract-based presentations; proceedings/publication pathways vary by year | Strong applied/industrial prestige, especially for standards and qualification | A good venue if the paper is framed around reliable powder delivery, reproducibility, calibration, and standardizable AM feedstock preparation infrastructure. Less ideal for the generative-CAD story, but strong for translation toward industrial AM practice. (low2025selfdrivinglaboratoriestranslating pages 7-7, sperry2023formationofeasytoremove pages 15-15) |
 26→| Strong | RAPID + TCT | SME in partnership with TCT | Major additive manufacturing event combining research, applications, and industry exhibition | Annual; typically spring in North America; location varies | Usually abstract-based conference presentations; many talks are non-archival | High visibility in industrial AM; academic selectivity moderate | Useful for exposure to AM practitioners and machine developers, especially if commercialization, user adoption, or system demos matter. It is less archival and less materials-discovery-centric than SFF, so it ranks below SFF for this paper. (sperry2023formationofeasytoremove pages 15-15) |
 27→| Stretch | CVPR Workshops relevant to 3D/CAD generation | IEEE/CVF CVPR workshop organizers | Computer vision, 3D generation, multimodal design, geometry, CAD-related generation/reconstruction | With CVPR, typically June; location follows host city | Workshop papers or extended abstracts; archival status varies by workshop | Selective and visible in ML/vision; fit depends heavily on workshop theme | Worth considering only for the generative-CAD pipeline, especially if you have clear technical novelty in LLM-to-parametric-CAD generation, multimodal conditioning, or CAD sequence modeling. The SDL powder-doser hardware itself will not be the main attraction. (chen2026learningfromdesign pages 9-11, li2025cadllamaleveraginglarge pages 1-2) |
 28→| Stretch | SIGGRAPH Technical Papers / ACM Transactions on Graphics pathway | ACM SIGGRAPH / ACM | Top-tier graphics, geometry processing, generative shape/CAD modeling, 3D representations | Annual; typically summer; rotating North American and international venues | Full technical paper; archival, highly selective | Elite venue in graphics/geometry | Relevant only if the generative-CAD component is a substantial standalone scientific contribution at the level of new CAD representations, B-rep generation, or high-impact geometry learning. For a hardware-first paper, this is mainly a stretch side-channel rather than a primary target. (wu2026aidrivengenerationof pages 35-36, usama2026nurbgenhighfidelitytexttocad pages 8-9) |
 29→| Stretch | Conference on Neural Information Processing Systems (NeurIPS) – main conference | NeurIPS Foundation | Core machine learning conference with theory, methods, and AI-for-science papers | Annual; typically December; rotating locations | Full archival conference paper | Extremely selective and prestigious | A stretch unless the work’s main novelty is an ML method, benchmark, or broadly interesting AI system rather than the doser hardware. The materials/SDL framing is better suited to the workshop ecosystem than to the main conference. (chen2026learningfromdesign pages 9-11, miret2026ai4maticlr2026iclr2026 pages 4-6) |
 30→| Stretch | ICML Workshops relevant to AI for Science / materials / design | Workshop organizers under the ICML program | ML methods, AI for science, scientific discovery, sometimes design/generative modeling | With ICML, typically July; location follows host city | Usually non-archival workshop papers or abstracts | Good visibility but workshop-level prestige | Potentially useful for the generative-CAD or autonomous-experimentation algorithmic aspects, but less hardware-amenable than SDL- or materials-specific meetings. Best as a side venue for the AI/design methodology, not the main apparatus paper. (chen2026learningfromdesign pages 9-11, miret2026ai4maticlr2026iclr2026 pages 2-4) |
 31→| Stretch | Gordon Research Conference on Combinatorial and High Throughput Materials Science | Gordon Research Conferences | Intensive, discussion-focused meeting on high-throughput and combinatorial materials science | Typically summer at residential venues; by application; often in North America/Europe | Application-based attendance; posters common; no formal archival proceedings | Very prestigious, intimate, and discussion-driven | Scientifically very strong for the alloy-discovery and high-throughput materials angle, but not ideal if your goal is a citable conference paper or broad hardware dissemination. Best for relationship-building with senior materials researchers once results are mature. (silva2024shapingthefuture pages 12-14) |
 32→| Stretch | International Conference on Learning Representations (ICLR) – main conference | ICLR organizing body | Core ML conference centered on representation learning and foundation models | Annual; typically spring; rotating location | Full archival conference paper via OpenReview | Extremely selective and prestigious | Like NeurIPS main, this is only realistic if the LLM/code-based CAD pipeline becomes the dominant scientific contribution and is competitive as ML research independent of the doser. The hardware SDL story by itself is not a natural fit. (miret2026ai4maticlr2026iclr2026 pages 4-6, miret2026ai4maticlr2026iclr2026 pages 2-4) |
 33→
 34→
 35→*Table: This table ranks the most relevant presentation venues for the open-hardware self-driving-lab multi-powder doser and its generative-CAD pipeline. It balances SDL/materials fit, additive-manufacturing relevance, and generative-CAD visibility while noting submission style and prestige.*
 36→
 37→---
 38→
 39→## Tier 1: Best-Fit Venues (Highest Priority)
 40→
 41→### 1. Accelerate Conference (Acceleration Consortium, University of Toronto)
 42→
 43→- **Scope:** The SDL community's flagship event, covering self-driving labs, materials acceleration platforms, open/democratized infrastructure, and autonomous experimentation workflows.
 44→- **Timing/Location:** Annual; the 2024 edition was held in Vancouver, BC (pelkie2025democratizingselfdrivinglabs pages 1-3).
 45→- **Submission format:** Workshop-style participation including talks, demonstrations, poster sessions, and community showcases. The "Democratizing Self-Driving Labs" workshop at Accelerate 2024 featured 14 contributed hardware/software demonstrations (pelkie2025democratizingselfdrivinglabs pages 1-3). Generally non-archival or lightly archival.
 46→- **Selectivity/Prestige:** High within the SDL community; field-specific rather than society-wide. The Acceleration Consortium is led by Alán Aspuru-Guzik at the University of Toronto (verifiable at https://acceleration.utoronto.ca/).
 47→- **Fit justification:** This is the single best audience for the doser. The Democratizing SDLs workshop explicitly celebrated user-developed hardware infrastructure, cost-driven open-source designs, and practical SDL building blocks (pelkie2025democratizingselfdrivinglabs pages 1-3). The multi-powder doser with its open-hardware, generative-CAD, and calibration contributions maps directly onto their themes.
 48→- **Official page:** https://acceleration.utoronto.ca/accelerate
 49→
 50→### 2. Faraday Discussions (RSC) — AI/Data-Driven Topics
 51→
 52→- **Scope:** The Royal Society of Chemistry's prestigious discussion-meeting series. A recent Faraday Discussion on "Data-driven discovery in the chemical sciences" (Vol. 256, 2025) covered ML, automation, robotics, and self-driving laboratories extensively (cooper2025concludingremarksfaraday pages 1-3, cooper2025concludingremarksfaraday pages 13-16, cooper2025concludingremarksfaraday pages 11-13). Papers on SDLs for optoelectronic materials have appeared in the same series (scragg2026).
 53→- **Timing/Location:** Topic-dependent scheduling; held in the UK and internationally. Meetings are small and curated.
 54→- **Submission format:** Full papers are submitted in advance and pre-published; oral discussion at the meeting is recorded and published alongside the papers in the *Faraday Discussions* journal (cooper2025concludingremarksfaraday pages 1-3, cooper2025concludingremarksfaraday pages 23-26). This is a distinctive, archival, high-prestige format.
 55→- **Selectivity/Prestige:** Very high. Faraday Discussions papers carry significant weight. Invitations are typically extended by the scientific committee.
 56→- **Fit justification:** Ideal if the paper emphasizes closed-loop calibration as an autonomous-experimentation infrastructure contribution, data-driven workflow design, or lessons for accelerating materials discovery. This aligns directly with the primary target journal *Digital Discovery* (RSC).
 57→- **Official page:** https://www.rsc.org/events/series/faraday-discussions
 58→
 59→### 3. MRS Fall Meeting (Materials Research Society)
 60→
 61→- **Scope:** The largest annual materials science conference, with recurring symposia on autonomous experimentation, materials informatics, AI-accelerated materials discovery, and advanced manufacturing (silva2024shapingthefuture pages 12-14, low2025selfdrivinglaboratoriestranslating pages 7-7).
 62→- **Timing/Location:** Late November/December, Boston, MA.
 63→- **Submission format:** Abstract-based oral talks and posters. Proceedings are uncommon for most symposia; contributions are typically non-archival at the conference level.
 64→- **Selectivity/Prestige:** Very high prestige in materials science.
 65→- **Fit justification:** Strong for the alloy-discovery and autonomous-experimentation framing. Look for symposia titled along the lines of "Autonomous Experimentation," "Materials Acceleration Platforms," or "AI for Materials." Hardware-rich contributions with compelling calibration data and an autonomous discovery use case can stand out.
 66→- **Official page:** https://www.mrs.org/meetings-events/fall-meeting
 67→
 68→### 4. Solid Freeform Fabrication (SFF) Symposium — An Additive Manufacturing Conference
 69→
 70→- **Scope:** The premier academic AM conference covering processes, feedstocks, machine development, materials characterization, and design for AM (sperry2023formationofeasytoremove pages 15-15).
 71→- **Timing/Location:** Annual, typically August, hosted by the University of Texas at Austin, Austin, TX.
 72→- **Submission format:** Full papers in open-access proceedings published by UT Austin. The proceedings are openly available and widely cited in the AM community.
 73→- **Selectivity/Prestige:** High prestige in AM research, particularly for technical depth.
 74→- **Fit justification:** Excellent for the AM hardware side of the project: powder handling, dosing precision, alloy-feedstock preparation, auger mechanisms, and integration into AM workflows. SFF is more accepting of apparatus, methods, and calibration-rich contributions than many other AM venues (sperry2023formationofeasytoremove pages 15-15).
 75→- **Official page:** https://www.sffsymposium.org/
 76→
 77→### 5. "Shaping the Future of Self-Driving Autonomous Laboratories" Workshop (ORNL / DOE)
 78→
 79→- **Scope:** A U.S. national-laboratory-focused workshop on SDL architectures, instrumentation integration, AI/ML decision-making, data standards, and ecosystem building (silva2024shapingthefuture pages 1-4, silva2024shapingthefuture pages 16-17, silva2024shapingthefuture pages 4-6).
 80→- **Timing/Location:** The inaugural edition was held November 7–8, 2024, in Denver, CO (silva2024shapingthefuture pages 16-17). Future continuity is plausible but not yet confirmed as a recurring series.
 81→- **Submission format:** Participation via talks, panels, and interactive breakout sessions. The workshop output is documented in an ORNL technical report (ORNL/TM-2024/3714) rather than archival proceedings (silva2024shapingthefuture pages 1-4).
 82→- **Selectivity/Prestige:** High strategic value; prestige is networking/roadmapping rather than publication-driven. Participants represent ORNL, SLAC, PNNL, CMU, NCSU, JHU, UTK, Sandia, and other institutions (silva2024shapingthefuture pages 4-6).
 83→- **Fit justification:** Outstanding for gaining feedback, collaborators, and visibility among national-lab infrastructure builders. The project's open hardware, multi-instrument integration, and calibration aspects align with the workshop's instrumentation and interoperability focus.
 84→- **Official page:** The 2024 report is at https://doi.org/10.2172/2481197
 85→
 86→---
 87→
 88→## Tier 2: Strong Venues
 89→
 90→### 6. TMS Annual Meeting & Exhibition
 91→- **Organizer:** The Minerals, Metals & Materials Society (TMS). **Timing:** Annually, Feb–Mar, rotating North American cities. **Format:** Abstract-based talks/posters; some symposia produce proceedings or journal special issues. **Prestige:** High in metallurgy and structural materials. **Fit:** Very strong for the alloy-discovery and metallurgy audience if the doser is positioned as enabling compositional exploration for powder-based AM alloys.
 92→- **Official page:** https://www.tms.org/AnnualMeeting
 93→
 94→### 7. MRS Spring Meeting
 95→- **Organizer:** Materials Research Society. **Timing:** Annually, April, often Phoenix, AZ. **Format:** Abstract-based; non-archival. **Prestige:** Very high. **Fit:** Similar to MRS Fall; look for spring symposia on autonomous experimentation or digital manufacturing.
 96→- **Official page:** https://www.mrs.org/meetings-events/spring-meeting
 97→
 98→### 8. ASME IDETC/CIE — Design Automation Conference (DAC) and Computers and Information in Engineering (CIE)
 99→- **Organizer:** American Society of Mechanical Engineers. **Timing:** Annually in August; 2025 edition was August 17–20 in Anaheim, CA (seng2025detc2025168839 pages 1-2). **Format:** Full archival papers in ASME conference proceedings. The DAC track (Vol. 3A/B) and CIE track (Vol. 2) are separate sub-conferences within the larger IDETC/CIE event (seng2025detc2025168839 pages 1-2). **Prestige:** Strong in engineering design and design automation. **Fit:** The best serious archival design-engineering venue for the generative-CAD half, especially if the story emphasizes the LLM/code-based CAD pipeline, parametric design generation, and reproducible hardware design automation. Recent work on generative CAD and AI copilots for design has appeared here.
100→- **Official page:** https://event.asme.org/IDETC-CIE
101→
102→### 9. AIChE Annual Meeting
103→- **Organizer:** American Institute of Chemical Engineers. **Timing:** Annually, November. **Format:** Abstract-based; mostly non-archival. **Prestige:** High in chemical/process engineering. **Fit:** Good for the dosing/dispensing and process-control framing, autonomous experimentation modules, or closed-loop optimization.
104→- **Official page:** https://www.aiche.org/annual
105→
106→### 10–11. AI4Mat Workshops at NeurIPS and ICLR
107→- **Organizer:** AI4Mat community (organizers include S. Miret, D. Circi, N.M.A. Krishnan, E. Jin) (miret2026ai4maticlr2026iclr2026 pages 4-6, miret2026ai4maticlr2026iclr2026 pages 1-2, miret2026ai4maticlr2026iclr2026 pages 2-4). **Timing:** AI4Mat-NeurIPS runs at NeurIPS in December; AI4Mat-ICLR runs at ICLR in spring. The series has run at NeurIPS 2022–2025 (growing from ~60 to ~250 participants) and at ICLR 2025–2026 (miret2026ai4maticlr2026iclr2026 pages 4-6, miret2026ai4maticlr2026iclr2026 pages 1-2). **Format:** Non-archival workshop papers via OpenReview (at least 3 reviewers per submission); submission categories include Papers, Tools & Findings, Tiny Papers (2-page extended abstracts), and themed tracks (miret2026ai4maticlr2026iclr2026 pages 4-6, miret2026ai4maticlr2026iclr2026 pages 1-2, miret2026ai4maticlr2026iclr2026 pages 2-4). **Prestige:** High visibility within the ML-for-materials community. **Fit:** Good if the paper foregrounds the autonomous materials-discovery framing, benchmark/data value, or the generative-CAD/AI pipeline. The "Tools & Findings" track is notably friendlier to infrastructure and hardware than most ML venues.
108→- **Official page:** https://sites.google.com/view/ai4mat (check for current edition URL)
109→
110→### 12. GOSH — Gathering for Open Science Hardware
111→- **Organizer:** Global Open Science Hardware community. **Timing:** Irregular but recurring global gatherings since 2016; locations have included Shenzhen (2018) and other international sites (arancio2021openingupthe pages 20-23). **Format:** Unconference/open-participation model; ~70% of the program is defined on-demand; includes hands-on workshops, building sessions, and community deliberation. Attendance is open, often with subsidized participation (arancio2021openingupthe pages 15-17, arancio2021openingupthe pages 10-12). No formal archival proceedings. **Prestige:** High within the open-hardware movement; low conventional academic selectivity. **Fit:** Excellent for the open-hardware dissemination mission, documentation feedback, and adoption. Few places are better for finding users and contributors for the doser design.
112→- **Official page:** https://openhardware.science/
113→
114→### 13. ASTM ICAM (International Conference on Additive Manufacturing)
115→- **Organizer:** ASTM International, with AM Center of Excellence partners. **Timing:** Annual, varying international locations. **Format:** Abstract-based presentations. **Prestige:** Strong for standards and qualification in AM. **Fit:** Good if framed around reliable powder delivery, reproducibility, and standardizable AM feedstock infrastructure.
116→- **Official page:** https://amcoe.org/icam
117→
118→### 14. RAPID + TCT
119→- **Organizer:** SME in partnership with TCT. **Timing:** Annual, typically spring, North America. **Format:** Abstract-based presentations; many talks are non-archival. Industry exhibition is a major component. **Prestige:** High visibility in industrial AM; moderate academic selectivity. **Fit:** Useful for exposure to AM practitioners and machine developers, especially if commercialization or system demos matter. Less archival than SFF.
120→- **Official page:** https://www.rapid3devent.com/
121→
122→---
123→
124→## Tier 3: Stretch Venues
125→
126→### 15. CVPR Workshops (3D/CAD Generation)
127→- **Organizer:** IEEE/CVF. **Timing:** Annually, typically June. **Format:** Workshop papers or extended abstracts; archival status varies. **Prestige:** High in ML/vision. **Fit:** Worth considering only for the generative-CAD pipeline, if there is clear technical novelty in LLM-to-parametric-CAD generation. CAD-Llama appeared at CVPR 2025 (li2025cadllamaleveraginglarge pages 1-2), and CADLoop appeared at a CVPR workshop (chen2026learningfromdesign pages 9-11). The SDL hardware itself will not be the main attraction.
128→- **Official page:** https://cvpr.thecvf.com/
129→
130→### 16. SIGGRAPH Technical Papers / ACM TOG
131→- **Organizer:** ACM SIGGRAPH. **Timing:** Annually, summer. **Format:** Full archival technical papers; highly selective (~25% acceptance). **Prestige:** Elite in graphics/geometry. BrepGen was published in ACM TOG (SIGGRAPH) (wu2026aidrivengenerationof pages 35-36, usama2026nurbgenhighfidelitytexttocad pages 8-9). **Fit:** Only if the generative-CAD component is a standalone scientific contribution at the level of new CAD representations or geometry generation methods.
132→- **Official page:** https://www.siggraph.org/
133→
134→### 17. NeurIPS Main Conference
135→- **Organizer:** NeurIPS Foundation. **Timing:** Annually, December. **Format:** Full archival conference paper; extremely selective (~20–25%). **Fit:** A stretch unless the work's main novelty is an ML method or benchmark. The workshop ecosystem (AI4Mat) is far more appropriate for this project.
136→- **Official page:** https://neurips.cc/
137→
138→### 18. ICML Workshops (AI for Science / Design)
139→- **Organizer:** ICML workshop organizers. **Timing:** Annually, July. **Format:** Non-archival workshop papers. **Fit:** Potentially useful for the generative-CAD or autonomous-experimentation algorithmic aspects, but less hardware-amenable than SDL-specific meetings.
140→- **Official page:** https://icml.cc/
141→
142→### 19. GRC on Combinatorial and High Throughput Materials Science
143→- **Organizer:** Gordon Research Conferences. **Timing:** Typically summer, at residential venues. **Format:** Application-based attendance; posters and short talks; no archival proceedings. **Prestige:** Very prestigious and intimate. **Fit:** Scientifically strong for the alloy-discovery and high-throughput materials angle, but not ideal if the goal is a citable output or broad hardware dissemination. Best for relationship-building once results are mature.
144→- **Official page:** https://www.grc.org/ (search for "Combinatorial and High Throughput Materials Science")
145→
146→### 20. ICLR Main Conference
147→- **Organizer:** ICLR. **Timing:** Annually, spring. **Format:** Full archival papers via OpenReview; extremely selective. **Fit:** Only realistic if the LLM/code-based CAD pipeline becomes the dominant contribution competitive as ML research. The hardware SDL story is not a natural fit for the main conference.
148→- **Official page:** https://iclr.cc/
149→
150→---
151→
152→## Strategic Recommendations
153→
154→1. **Lead with SDL-focused venues.** The Accelerate Conference and the ORNL SDL workshop series are the most natural homes for the integrated hardware + autonomous-experimentation story. These communities explicitly value open-hardware, user-developed automation infrastructure, and practical SDL building blocks (pelkie2025democratizingselfdrivinglabs pages 1-3, silva2024shapingthefuture pages 1-4).
155→
156→2. **Target MRS Fall and SFF for materials and AM credibility.** For peer recognition in materials science and additive manufacturing, an MRS Fall symposium on autonomous experimentation or an SFF paper on the AM hardware side provides high visibility and archival value (sperry2023formationofeasytoremove pages 15-15).
157→
158→3. **Use ASME IDETC/CIE for the generative-CAD angle.** The DAC and CIE tracks within IDETC/CIE are the most appropriate archival engineering-design venues for the LLM/code-based generative-CAD pipeline (seng2025detc2025168839 pages 1-2). Recent work on AI copilots for design, CAD-prompted generative models, and generative statistically self-similar structures has appeared here.
159→
160→4. **Consider Faraday Discussions for a polished, high-prestige presentation.** If a future Faraday Discussion is announced on automation, AI for materials, or data-driven discovery, the project would fit well as a pre-submitted paper emphasizing the closed-loop calibration and autonomous discovery infrastructure (cooper2025concludingremarksfaraday pages 1-3, cooper2025concludingremarksfaraday pages 23-26).
161→
162→5. **Use AI4Mat workshop "Tools & Findings" tracks for ML-community visibility.** The AI4Mat workshops at NeurIPS and ICLR have specific tracks for tools, infrastructure, and findings that are more accommodating of hardware-rich contributions than typical ML venues (miret2026ai4maticlr2026iclr2026 pages 4-6, miret2026ai4maticlr2026iclr2026 pages 1-2, miret2026ai4maticlr2026iclr2026 pages 2-4).
163→
164→6. **GOSH for community building and adoption.** While GOSH does not produce archival papers, it is unmatched for building a user community around the open-hardware doser design and getting feedback on documentation, reproducibility, and accessibility (arancio2021openingupthe pages 15-17, arancio2021openingupthe pages 10-12).
165→
166→7. **Stretch ML/graphics venues only with a strong standalone ML contribution.** CVPR, SIGGRAPH, NeurIPS, and ICLR main conferences are realistic only if the generative-CAD pipeline yields novel ML methodology that stands on its own. For LLM-based CAD generation, CVPR workshops and AAAI have been receptive (chen2026learningfromdesign pages 9-11, usama2026nurbgenhighfidelitytexttocad pages 7-8, li2025cadllamaleveraginglarge pages 1-2).
167→
168→---
169→

171→

Public verification of individuals mentioned in this document (e.g., Alán Aspuru-Guzik, Andrew Cooper, Santiago Miret) can be confirmed via their institutional faculty pages, Google Scholar profiles, or the official conference/workshop pages cited above. No personal contact information was found to be necessary beyond the institutional and conference pages already provided.

messages.name: read

messages.content:
1→| Rank | Venue full name | Publisher | Approximate Impact Factor / CiteScore / h5-index | Scope summary | Accepts applied hardware/SDL contribution? | Acceptance bar | Representative recent generative-CAD paper (2022-2025) | Fit justification |
 2→|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
 3→| 1 | Digital Discovery | Royal Society of Chemistry (RSC) | IF ~6.2; CiteScore ~10 | Publishes digital, automated, AI-enabled chemistry/materials workflows, including self-driving labs, open hardware, and low-cost automation. | Yes | Medium | Yoshikawa et al., “Digital pipette: open hardware for liquid transfer in self-driving laboratories” (2023); Doloi et al., “Democratizing Self-Driving Labs: Advances in Low-Cost 3D Printing for Laboratory Automation” (2025) (pqac-00000013, pqac-00000008) | Best overall fit if the paper foregrounds the open-hardware SDL contribution and treats generative-CAD as the enabling design methodology. It already publishes SDL hardware, 3D-printed lab automation, and community-facing open systems papers, so a multi-powder doser for autonomous AM alloy discovery is squarely in-scope. |
 4→| 2 | Additive Manufacturing | Elsevier | IF ~11; CiteScore ~19 | Top AM journal covering materials, processes, design-for-AM, AM systems, and integrated design/manufacturing workflows. | Partial | High | AM-driven generative design / DfAM papers are common; e.g., ML-assisted DfAM and topology/generative-design studies cited in the AM literature review stream (Watson 2023 contextual fit via AM-focused generative design) | Highest-impact manufacturing venue still plausibly amenable if the paper emphasizes the doser as novel AM-enabling hardware for alloy discovery and shows that the generative-CAD pipeline materially improved printability, modularity, or DfAM performance. Less ideal if the manuscript is mostly about SDL architecture rather than AM outcomes. |
 5→| 3 | The International Journal of Advanced Manufacturing Technology | Springer Nature | IF ~3.4; CiteScore ~8 | Broad manufacturing journal spanning design, process planning, automation, robotics, CAD/CAM, and applied AI in manufacturing. | Yes | Medium-High | Daareyni et al., “Generative AI meets CAD: enhancing engineering design to manufacturing processes with large language models” (2025); Watson et al., “Generative design of space frames for additive manufacturing technology” (2023) (pqac-00000017, pqac-00000018) | Very strong fit for a paper that integrates LLM-driven code-based CAD with actual fabrication hardware and manufacturing validation. It has direct precedent for OpenSCAD/LLM CAD pipelines and for generative-design-for-AM papers, making it one of the safest journal targets. |
 6→| 4 | Journal of Mechanical Design | ASME | IF ~3.3; CiteScore ~6 | Flagship engineering-design journal focused on methods, theories, and systems for design synthesis, automation, and computational design. | Partial | High | Regenwetter et al., “Deep Generative Models in Engineering Design: A Review” (2022); GenCAD=3D (2025) noted in recent JMD-linked literature (pqac-00000019) | Excellent fit if the paper makes a genuine engineering-design-method contribution: e.g., an agentic generative-CAD workflow with verification, parameterized modularity, and DfAM-aware design rules validated on the doser. It is less suitable if the main novelty is the hardware build itself rather than the design methodology. |
 7→| 5 | Journal of Computing and Information Science in Engineering (JCISE) | ASME | IF ~3.1; CiteScore ~6 | Covers computational methods, CAD/CAE/CAM, design automation, geometric computing, and AI/ML for engineering information and systems. | Partial | Medium-High | Recent JCISE/JMD-linked LLM4CAD and CAD-automation literature cluster; code-generation/engineering informatics framing fits the venue’s computational-design remit (pqac-00000019) | Strong choice when the core claim is a reusable computational pipeline for text/code-based CAD generation, verification, and design-space exploration, with the hardware as the case study. More amenable than pure ML venues, but still expects a clear computational contribution. |
 8→| 6 | Computer-Aided Design | Elsevier | IF ~3.0; CiteScore ~7 | Core CAD journal for geometric modeling, design automation, computational representations, and CAD-related AI methods. | Partial | High | Simplification of 3D CAD models using GANs (2023, cited as unobtainable in search); broader generative-CAD lineage including B-rep/sequence modeling is central to the venue ecosystem (pqac-00000000, pqac-00000006) | Strong venue if the manuscript leans into the generative-CAD pipeline itself: code representations, geometry-kernel verification, parametric editability, and CAD automation. Weaker if the paper is mainly an SDL/open-hardware system paper with minimal CAD-method novelty. |
 9→| 7 | Advanced Engineering Informatics | Elsevier | IF ~8.0; CiteScore ~15 | Publishes AI, knowledge-based systems, digital engineering, and informatics for design, construction, and manufacturing. | Yes | High | Generative/AI-for-design and engineering-informatics papers are common; recent agentic/LLM engineering design pipelines are highly consonant with venue norms | Very attractive if the paper stresses the end-to-end digital thread: LLM-driven CAD, validation, knowledge capture, hardware documentation, and closed-loop laboratory deployment. It is more systems-friendly than pure CAD journals, while still rewarding methodological novelty. |
10→| 8 | Computers in Industry | Elsevier | IF ~10.0; CiteScore ~17 | Focuses on industrially relevant computer-based methods, digitalization, automation, and deployable systems in engineering/manufacturing. | Yes | High | Applied AI/CAD/manufacturing systems papers are typical; recent industrial CAD-generation and automation work fits its translational orientation | Good high-impact option if the doser is presented as a deployable industrial-style digital engineering system rather than a purely academic design-method paper. Better for demonstrable workflow integration and usability than for algorithmic CAD novelty per se. |
11→| 9 | Proceedings of the Design Society | Cambridge University Press / Design Society | archival proceedings; no standard JIF; CiteScore modest | Archival design-research proceedings covering engineering design methods, tools, and emerging AI-enabled design workflows. | Yes | Medium | Schüpbach et al., “From text to design: a framework to leverage LLM agents for automated CAD generation” (2025) (pqac-00000016) | One of the clearest venues for agentic generative-CAD workflows with a real engineering case study. Lower impact than the best journals, but notably more amenable to an applied paper where the methodological contribution is modest yet meaningful. |
12→| 10 | Computer-Aided Design and Applications | CAD Solutions / U-turn Press | IF ~0.8; CiteScore ~2 | Application-oriented CAD journal publishing practical CAD methods, implementations, and case studies. | Yes | Medium-Low | Shao & Shao, “Innovation of Apparel Design Based on Deep Generative Modeling and Its Practice of CAD Collaborative Design” (2024) | Plausible outlet for a pragmatic code-based generative-CAD pipeline tied to a hardware case study, especially if the contribution is more implementation-oriented than theoretically deep. Lower prestige, but comparatively forgiving on applied systems work. |
13→| 11 | HardwareX | Elsevier | IF ~2.5; CiteScore ~4 | Dedicated open-hardware journal for detailed, reproducible scientific hardware descriptions with build files and validation. | Yes | Medium | Laborem Box (2022) and other open-source lab platforms; not generative-CAD-specific, but directly aligned with publishable hardware documentation (pqac-00000015) | Best venue if the primary goal is to disseminate the open hardware itself with files, BOM, and validation. However, it is not a high-impact generative-CAD venue, so it is better as a companion paper than as the main flagship publication for the CAD/AI angle. |
14→| 12 | Applied Sciences | MDPI | IF ~2.5; CiteScore ~4 | Broad applied-engineering journal that accepts practical AI/manufacturing/CAD applications across many domains. | Yes | Low-Medium | Yüksel & Börklü, “A generative deep learning approach for improving the mechanical performance of structural components” (2024, cited in search results) | Reasonably amenable to applied system papers and fast-moving AI/manufacturing work, but less selective and less field-defining. Useful fallback if the work is interdisciplinary but does not fit a sharper design/manufacturing venue. |
15→| 13 | ACM Transactions on Graphics (SIGGRAPH / SIGGRAPH Asia) | ACM | IF ~6.2; h5-index aligned with SIGGRAPH prestige | Premier graphics venue for fundamental advances in geometry, graphics, and 3D generation. | No | Very High | Xu et al., “BrepGen: A B-rep Generative Diffusion Model with Structured Latent Geometry” (2024) (pqac-00000006) | Extremely high prestige for generative-CAD methods, but a poor fit for an applied SDL hardware paper that merely uses such methods. Acceptance would require a substantial new generative geometry/modeling algorithm, not just a strong hardware deployment. |
16→| 14 | IEEE/CVF Conference on Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition (CVPR) | IEEE / CVF | h5-index ~389 | Top computer-vision conference publishing novel vision and 3D/CAD generation methods. | No | Very High | Li et al., “CAD-Llama: Leveraging Large Language Models for Computer-Aided Design Parametric 3D Model Generation” (2025) (pqac-00000003) | CVPR clearly accepts parametric CAD generation, but only when the core contribution is a new ML/vision method. An applied multi-powder doser paper using generative CAD as a design tool would almost certainly not clear the bar. |
17→| 15 | International Conference on Learning Representations (ICLR) | OpenReview | h5-index ~286 | Leading ML conference for representation learning and foundation-model methodology. | No | Very High | Seek-CAD / OBJ2CAD-style text-to-CAD work appears in the ICLR orbit or under review, but not as applied hardware papers (pqac-00000007) | Not suitable unless the paper advances core model architecture, alignment, or training methodology for generative CAD. The applied hardware/SDL framing works against, not for, acceptance. |
18→| 16 | Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems (NeurIPS) | NeurIPS Foundation | h5-index ~278 | Premier ML venue focused on novel algorithms, theory, and foundational systems for machine learning. | No | Very High | Text2CAD (NeurIPS 2024) as noted in the CAD venue evidence summary (pqac-00000001, pqac-00000005) | Like CVPR, NeurIPS is relevant to generative CAD only when the contribution is primarily a new ML method, dataset, or benchmark. An applied hardware paper that uses LLM CAD would not be competitive. |
19→| 17 | International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML) | ML Research Press | h5-index ~228 | Top ML conference for methodological advances in machine learning. | No | Very High | SkexGen is cited in the ICML-linked generative-CAD literature stream (pqac-00000005, pqac-00000006) | Very poor fit for the proposed paper as stated. ICML would only make sense if the generative-CAD pipeline itself introduced a broadly important ML advance. |
20→| 18 | International Conference on Computer Vision / European Conference on Computer Vision (ICCV / ECCV) | IEEE / CVF / Springer | h5-index ~200+ class | Major vision conferences publishing 3D understanding, reconstruction, and CAD-related learning methods. | No | Very High | DeepCAD and related CAD-sequence modeling appear in the vision-conference ecosystem; CAD-generation has precedent, but as method papers (pqac-00000000, pqac-00000004) | Similar story to CVPR: the venue family is appropriate for parametric CAD generation research, but not for an applied SDL/hardware manuscript unless the main novelty is a new vision/3D learning method. |
21→
22→
23→*Table: This table ranks peer-reviewed venues for a generative-CAD plus open-hardware self-driving-lab paper, prioritizing fit for an applied hardware/SDL contribution over raw venue prestige. It distinguishes journals and conferences that are realistically receptive from high-impact ML/graphics venues that publish generative CAD but would likely reject an applied systems paper.*
messages.name: read

messages.content:
1→| Tier | Venue Full Name | Organizer | Scope | Typical Timing/Location | Submission Format | Selectivity/Prestige | Fit Justification |
 2→|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
 3→| Best-Fit | Accelerate Conference | Acceleration Consortium, University of Toronto | Self-driving labs, materials acceleration platforms, autonomous experimentation, community demos/workshops | Annual; recent editions in Toronto/Vancouver; often summer-fall; exact future location/year can vary | Typically abstract/poster/demo/workshop-style participation; usually non-archival or lightly archival; workshop/demonstration friendly | High relevance inside SDL community; prestige is field-specific rather than society-wide | Probably the single best audience for an open-hardware SDL doser because the community already values low-cost, user-developed automation infrastructure, calibration workflows, and democratized hardware. It is also unusually receptive to system demonstrations and practical infrastructure rather than only algorithmic novelty. (pqac-00000000, pqac-00000001) |
 4→| Best-Fit | Faraday Discussions (especially data-driven discovery / AI-for-chemistry-materials topics) | Royal Society of Chemistry (RSC) | High-level themed meetings on chemistry/materials topics; for this project, data-driven discovery, automation, AI, and self-driving-lab adjacent themes are most relevant | Topic-dependent; held in the UK and internationally; cadence depends on theme rather than one fixed annual slot | Distinctive pre-submitted paper plus oral discussion model; archival in *Faraday Discussions* with discussion record published | Very prestigious, small, curated, discussion-intensive | Best if the manuscript is framed around closed-loop gravimetric calibration, autonomous materials-discovery infrastructure, and lessons for data-driven experimentation. Less ideal for purely mechanical build details, but excellent for a polished SDL systems paper aligned with *Digital Discovery* and RSC audiences. (pqac-00000016, pqac-00000021, pqac-00000022) |
 5→| Best-Fit | Materials Research Society (MRS) Fall Meeting | Materials Research Society | Broad materials meeting with recurring symposia on materials informatics, autonomous experimentation, AI for materials, and advanced manufacturing | Annual; typically late Nov/Dec in Boston, MA | Usually abstract-based talks/posters; archival proceedings uncommon for most symposia | Very high prestige in materials science; broad and visible | Strong fit for the alloy-discovery and SDL materials angle, especially if submitted to a symposium on autonomous experimentation, materials acceleration, or manufacturing/data infrastructure. Hardware-heavy AM dosing can stand out if coupled to compelling calibration data and a clear autonomous-discovery use case. (pqac-00000008, pqac-00000010) |
 6→| Best-Fit | Solid Freeform Fabrication Symposium (SFF Symposium) – An Additive Manufacturing Conference | The University of Texas at Austin | Deeply technical additive manufacturing conference spanning processes, feedstocks, design, materials, and machine development | Annual; typically August; Austin, Texas | Full paper in proceedings; archival/openly accessible conference papers are a norm for many contributions | High prestige in AM research, especially for technical depth and engineering rigor | Excellent venue for the AM hardware side: powder handling, dosing precision, alloy-feedstock preparation, and integration into AM workflows. It is better than most AM venues for a detailed engineering paper because the audience accepts apparatus, methods, and calibration-rich contributions. (pqac-00000015) |
 7→| Best-Fit | Shaping the Future of Self-Driving Autonomous Laboratories Workshop | Oak Ridge National Laboratory (ORNL) / DOE-lab ecosystem | National-lab-focused workshop on SDL architectures, instrumentation integration, AI/ML decision-making, data standards, workforce, and ecosystem building | Recent documented edition: Nov. 7–8, 2024, Denver, CO; future continuity is plausible but specific series recurrence is unverified | Workshop participation via talks/panels/breakouts; non-archival meeting format documented in technical report | High strategic relevance; prestige is networking/roadmapping rather than publication-driven | Outstanding fit if the goal is feedback, collaborators, and visibility among national-lab and infrastructure builders. The project’s open hardware, multi-instrument integration, and calibration/control aspects align especially well with the workshop’s instrumentation, interoperability, and standards focus. (pqac-00000004, pqac-00000005, pqac-00000006) |
 8→| Strong | The Minerals, Metals & Materials Society (TMS) Annual Meeting & Exhibition | TMS | Metals, alloys, ICME, process metallurgy, manufacturing, and data-enabled materials engineering | Annual; typically February-March; rotating North American cities | Usually abstract-based presentations; some tracks lead to proceedings or journal special issues | High prestige in metallurgy and structural materials | Very strong for the alloy-discovery and metallurgy audience, especially if the doser is positioned as enabling compositional exploration for powder-based AM alloys. Less naturally open-hardware-focused than SDL meetings, but stronger on alloy relevance than many automation venues. (pqac-00000008, pqac-00000009) |
 9→| Strong | Materials Research Society (MRS) Spring Meeting | Materials Research Society | Broad materials conference with symposia on informatics, automation, energy materials, and emerging fabrication methods | Annual; typically April; often Phoenix, AZ or Seattle, WA | Usually abstract-only talk/poster submissions; mostly non-archival | Very high prestige in materials science | Similar benefits to MRS Fall, though often a bit less central for some physical-metallurgy communities. A good option if there is a spring symposium specifically on autonomous experimentation, materials acceleration, or digital manufacturing. (pqac-00000008, pqac-00000010) |
10→| Strong | ASME International Design Engineering Technical Conferences & Computers and Information in Engineering Conference (IDETC/CIE), especially DAC and CIE tracks | American Society of Mechanical Engineers (ASME) | Engineering design, design automation, computational design, CAD/CAM, engineering informatics, AI-enabled design methods | Annual; typically August; recent documented edition Aug. 17–20, 2025 in Anaheim, CA | Full papers in ASME conference proceedings; archival | Strong prestige in engineering design and design automation | Best venue for the generative-CAD half if the story emphasizes the LLM/code-based CAD pipeline, parametric design generation, and reproducible hardware design automation. It is not the strongest SDL venue, but it is the best serious archival design-engineering venue on your list for the CAD contribution. (pqac-00000014) |
11→| Strong | AIChE Annual Meeting | American Institute of Chemical Engineers (AIChE) | Chemical engineering, process systems, laboratory automation, robotics, optimization, digitalization, autonomous experimentation | Annual; typically November; rotating U.S. cities | Usually abstract-based conference talks; some invited or extended submissions; mostly non-archival | High prestige in chemical/process engineering | Good fit if the work is framed as a dosing/dispensing platform, process-control problem, or autonomous experimentation module rather than primarily as AM hardware. Hardware rigor and closed-loop calibration can resonate, but AM-alloy relevance may be less central than at TMS/SFF. (pqac-00000016, pqac-00000010) |

function.name: run_cell

info.name: run_cell
info.description:
Run code in a notebook cell and return the execution output.

This method allows running code in a new cell (append) or re-running
an existing cell with updated code.

Usage Examples:
    run_cell("print('Hello, world!')",
            display_text="Creating example code") # Run code in new cell
    run_cell("print('Hello, world!')",
            display_text="Modifying example code",
            idx=0) # Run code in existing cell at index 0

Error Recovery:
    When a cell fails with an error, you MUST fix it by calling run_cell
    with the corrected code and the SAME idx as the failed cell:

    run_cell("corrected_code", idx=3)  # Fix error in Cell #3

    The cell number is shown in the output prefix (e.g., "[Cell #3]").
    Do NOT create a new cell to fix an error - always edit the failed cell.

display_text.description:
A short, ~100 character gerund phrase describing
the code intent for the end user.
display_text.title: Display Text

idx.description:
Cell index to run. If None or >= len(cells), appends a new cell.
If provided, updates and re-runs the existing cell at that index.
Use this to fix errors in existing cells.
idx.title: Idx

info.name: reset_kernel
info.description:
Reset the kernel to a clean state.

This clears all variables and execution state while preserving notebook history.

_unused.description:
Unused. Present so the tool schema has a property — OpenAI's
Responses API rejects zero-arg tools with "object schema missing
properties".
_unused.title: Unused

info.name: submit_answer
info.description: Submit your response to the research question.

answer.description: Your final response to the research question
answer.title: Answer

info.name: read
info.description:
Read file contents with line numbers.

Reads text files, source code, config files, images, PDFs, PowerPoint,
Word documents, and Jupyter notebooks. For binary data files (Excel,
databases, Parquet, etc.), use Python code instead.

limit.description: Maximum number of lines/pages to read
limit.title: Limit

offset.description:
Start line (0-based) for text, or start page/slide (1-based) for PDFs/PowerPoint/
Word documents
offset.title: Offset

info.name: explore
info.description:
Search the workspace or memory for information.

Spawns a lightweight sub-agent that can read files, grep, jq, and
run shell pipelines to find information in the workspace. Use this
instead of reading large files or exploring directories directly —
the sub-agent manages its own context so your context is not consumed.

What the sub-agent CAN do: list directories, read text and multimedia
files (PDFs, powerpoints, images), grep/jq/filter file contents, and
retrieve prior conversation history that has scrolled out of your context.

What the sub-agent CANNOT do: execute Python or R, modify the filesystem,
invoke external binaries (unzip, CLI tools, package installs), or perform
numerical computation or statistical analysis — those are your job with
`run_cell`.

Note: the sub-agent is a weaker model than you, so focus on using it for
information retrieval, but not complex analysis, reasoning, or decision-making.
You should be responsible for those activities, based on information the
sub-agent retrieves at your direction. If it reports that a result is not
stored on disk, that describes the workspace — run the computation yourself
with `run_cell` rather than treating it as a stop condition.

Common uses:
- Retrieve information from conversation memory after compaction or reset.
- Search large log or data files for specific entries
- Summarize contents of files you haven't read yet
- Retrieve outputs or conversation history from a completed sub-agent
  task (look under `<project_tag>/<trajectory_id>/`, which contains
  the sub-agent's files and its own `memory/`).

query.description:
What to find or summarize, e.g. "What datasets have I
downloaded so far?" or "Have I seen error X before, and how did I solve it?".
query.title: Query

info.name: view_docs
info.description:
Look up e14c API documentation for one or more modules, classes, or functions.

Browse top-down: start with "e14c" to see available modules, then
drill into specific modules. Use verbose=False to navigate the tree;
only use verbose=True on specific targets you've already identified.
Do not call view_docs speculatively at the start of a task — call it
just-in-time as you need specific APIs. Batch related lookups into a
single call, e.g. ``view_docs(["e14c.filesystem", "e14c.shell"])``.

verbose.description: If False, compact listing. If True, full signatures and docstrings.
verbose.title: Verbose

info.name: load_skill
info.description:
Load a skill guide by name.

Returns the full content of the skill's SKILL.md file, which
contains instructions, code examples, and best practices for
using the skill.

skill_name.description: Name of the skill to load (e.g. "edison").
skill_name.title: Skill Name

messages.content:
You are a rigorous data analysis agent with deep expertise in statistics, data science, and quantitative methods. Your primary directive is to provide accurate, evidence-based analysis in Jupyter notebooks while maintaining the highest standards of scientific integrity.

## Core Principles

**Do not fabricate data for any reason**

You must never invent, simulate, or fabricate data under any circumstances. All analyses, visualizations, and interpretations must be directly derivable from the provided dataset or data correctly pulled in from external sources (eg. gene annotations, external databases). If you cannot access required data you must report this limitation and end the analysis. You must not subsample data without an analytical or technical purpose. If the data must be subsampled due to memory limitations or other technical constraints, this must be justified and reported.

**All analyses must demonstrate statistical rigor and methodological excellence**

Every analytical procedure must be statistically sound and suitable for the specific data type and research question being addressed. You should always consider and mention underlying statistical assumptions (normality, independence, homoscedasticity, etc.). You must check assumptions before applying statistical tests and report when assumptions are violated. You should use correct statistical terminology, notation, and precision in reporting. You must report relevant metrics: p-values, confidence intervals, effect sizes, test statistics, degrees of freedom. You should apply appropriate corrections for multiple comparisons when necessary. You must distinguish between correlation and causation, avoiding causal claims from observational data.

**Report the limitations of the data and your analysis**

If a request is beyond your capabilities or the scope of provided data, you must state this clearly and concisely. You should never attempt to answer questions requiring domain knowledge you do not possess or cannot acquire through use of tools such as external data sources or web search. You must acknowledge limitations of methods, sample sizes, and data quality. You should communicate uncertainty and confidence levels explicitly.

**Never fabricate solutions when you cannot complete a task**

If you cannot do something, you must never fabricate a solution. You should clearly state what you cannot do and why, rather than providing false or misleading information.

**Be concise and focused in your analysis**

You should address the research question directly and efficiently. You must avoid extraneous information that doesn't contribute to answering the question. You should present findings with appropriate statistical precision (don't over-report decimal places). You must provide concrete, quantitative evidence with specific values that support or refute hypotheses.

Your environment has multiple CPUs and ample RAM.

You are operating in a fully-equipped production environment with significant computational resources, internet access, and all necessary tools for advanced data analysis, API calls, and data retrieval. You do NOT have access to GPUs or any other specialized hardware and you are limited to 3600.0 seconds of runtime.

## Error Response Protocol

When you cannot fulfill a request:
You must state clearly: "I cannot [specific request] because [specific limitation]"
You should explain: Brief explanation of the constraint or missing requirement
You must end analysis: Do not attempt workarounds that compromise data integrity
You should specify needs: If applicable, state what would be required to address the request properly

## Structured Analysis Protocol

Step 1: Define Analysis Plan

Outline specific data filtering, processing, and analysis steps
State the statistical methods and tests you will use
Identify potential limitations or assumptions
Example format: "1. Filter dataset for [criteria]. 2. Apply [transformation]. 3. Execute [statistical test]. 4. Interpret results against [threshold/criterion]."

Step 2: Execute the analysis plan

Execute your plan systematically, one step at a time
Follow closely the jupyter notebook implementation standards and error response protocol

Step 3: Present Quantitative Evidence

**Important: Use the submit_answer tool to respond to the research question. YOUR TASK IS NOT COMPLETE UNTIL AN ANSWER IS SUBMITTED**
Present findings with concrete, quantitative evidence
Provide specific values that define relationships or rules
Include relevant statistical metrics (correlation coefficients, p-values, effect sizes, fold changes)
Ensure evidence directly supports or refutes the research question



IMPORTANT: The core principles must be adhered to at all times. When in doubt, rather than proceeding with questionable analysis, make note of your uncertainty both in the notebook and in the answer. Scientific integrity requires absolute honesty about what can and cannot be determined from available data. It is always better to provide a limited but accurate analysis than to compromise data fidelity or statistical rigor.

# How You Work

You are Heron. You make discoveries, mostly alone. Novel, actionable, interesting — or you haven't done your job.

## How to Think

Match effort to the ask. Simple user queries get quick, direct answers — no over-planning, no cosmetic polish, no deliberating over themes or formatting. If the user asks a question you can answer now, answer it in the most direct way possible. Overthinking simple requests wastes the user's time and your steps.

Test with data first: Edison analysis agents and your own code can check hypotheses against real datasets right now.
An Edison literature review that doesn't lead to a test is procrastination.

Contain excitement. The ideation-execution gap: ideas that sound novel fall apart in practice. For every idea — what goes wrong? What's the cheapest test? Is it novel?

## Fighting Mode Collapse

RLHF makes your output clean, hedged, pleasant. This poisons your thinking because your notes become your future thoughts.

Write messy. Half-formed ideas stay half-formed. "This feels connected to X but I can't see how" > a tidy bullet point.
Hold claims provisionally true. "CLAIM: X causes Y" — push on it, see what breaks. Hedging kills momentum.
Seek new information. Social media. The date. Sideways citations. People who are wrong in interesting ways.

## How to Write

Don't write LLM slop. Write like a researcher in a hurry:

Don't use words like "delve," "moreover", or "embark." Use plain, specific language instead.

LLM writing puffs with arbitrary phrases like "marking a pivotal moment," "a significant shift," or "part of a broader movement." If something is important, show it through concrete detail, not grandiose framing.

Don't use sales writing. Avoid em dashes and the "rule of three" pattern. And the habit of opening with corrective constructions like "Not only X, but also Y" or "It's not just X — it's Y."

Don't hedge with false balance or vague qualifiers. If nuance is needed, make it specific rather than reflexive.

Write like a person, not a press release. Vary sentence length and structure. Use contractions. Some sentences are short. Avoid wrapping every paragraph in a tidy intro-body-conclusion arc, and resist the urge to summarize at the end with "In summary" or "Overall." Real writing is a little messy — that's what makes it human.

## Citing Sources

Always cite sources for claims, either to a primary source or specific dataset/analysis.

## Vibe

Direct. The research speaks or it doesn't. Some humor.

# Output Format

Once you have completed your analysis, submit your answer using the submit_answer tool.
Your answer must satisfy the following requirements including an answer and a decision list:

[Your complete answer]

[A bulleted list of discretionary analytical decisions made during the analysis]

**Discretionary decisions** are choices where:

- Multiple scientifically valid approaches exist
- Different researchers might reasonably choose different options
- The choice does not invalidate the analysis but may affect exact numerical results
- The decision reflects methodological judgment rather than correction of an error

Examples include:

- Statistical test selection when assumptions are borderline (e.g., "Used Mann-Whitney U test rather than t-test due to slight deviation from normality")
- Multiple testing correction method (e.g., "Applied FDR correction rather than Bonferroni")
- Significance thresholds (e.g., "Set alpha = 0.05")
- Quality control cutoffs (e.g., "Filtered genes with <10 reads across all samples")
- Normalization approach (e.g., "Applied log2 transformation before analysis")
- Database/reference version (e.g., "Used ENSEMBL v110 for gene annotations")
- Parameter values for algorithms (e.g., "Set k=5 for k-means clustering based on elbow method")
- Binning or grouping strategies for continuous variables
- Choice between multiple valid visualization scales (linear vs. log)

DO NOT include:

- Standard analytical steps that have no alternatives
- Corrections of errors or bugs
- Choices that are dictated by the data structure itself
- Software version numbers (unless the choice between versions was discretionary)

## R Code Execution (if needed)

If the task requires R code, use the `%%Rscript` cell magic. This environment uses a Python kernel, so R code must be run via this magic:

```
%%Rscript
library(tidyverse)
df <- read_csv("data.csv")
summary(df)
```

The entire cell content after `%%Rscript` is executed as R code via Rscript subprocess.

**Python↔R Data Exchange:** If you need to pass data between Python and R, use Parquet files:

- Python: `df.to_parquet("data.parquet")`
- R: `df <- arrow::read_parquet("data.parquet")`

**Displaying R Plots:** R plots are not automatically captured. To display plots:

1. Save the plot to a file in R:

   ```r
   %%Rscript
   library(ggplot2)
   p <- ggplot(data, aes(x, y)) + geom_point()
   ggsave("plot.png", p, width=8, height=6, dpi=150)
   ```

2. Display the saved image in a Python cell:

   ```python
   from IPython.display import Image, display

   display(Image("plot.png"))
   ```

**R Code Style (when using R):**

- Suppress startup messages: `suppressPackageStartupMessages(library(tidyverse))`
- Use explicit namespaces: `dplyr::select()` instead of `select()`
- Use ggplot2 for plots

## Workspace structure

Your own outputs belong in `$TRAJECTORY_WORKDIR` — an absolute path the runtime has already created for you, with any files from `data_storage_uris` downloaded there. **Write your outputs there using absolute paths** (e.g. `f"{os.environ['TRAJECTORY_WORKDIR']}/results.csv"`). A bare relative path like `results.csv` lands in `/workspace/` and pollutes the shared root.

Treat files outside your own subdir as read-only. Do not overwrite orchestrator files or sibling sub-agents' outputs.

Input files referenced in the orchestrator's query (e.g. `/workspace/<project_tag>/input.csv`) are on the same filesystem — open them directly, no `data_storage_uris` round-trip needed.

Your dispatched project tag is available as the `PROJECT_TAG` environment variable. `upload()` and `submit_task()` use it automatically — omit `project_tag` when you call them. Passing a value that disagrees with `PROJECT_TAG` raises `ValueError` rather than silently overriding, so your uploads stay grouped under the tag your orchestrator is tracking.

Your output subdir contains a `memory/` subdirectory with files named `*_memory_*.jsonl`. These are internal system files that automatically record your conversation history for recovery purposes. Do NOT read these files during normal operation—they contain raw message logs, not task data or context. If your context window is compacted (earlier messages become unavailable), you will receive explicit instructions on how to search these files to recover information. Until then, ignore the `memory/` directory entirely.

# Verification Checkpoints

Before submitting your answer, verify:

- [ ] All claims supported by data shown in notebook
- [ ] Statistical assumptions checked and reported
- [ ] Limitations explicitly stated
- [ ] No banned words or puffery in answer
- [ ] Quantitative evidence with specific values
- [ ] Discretionary decisions documented
- [ ] All citations are real and traceable (not fabricated)
- [ ] Quoted text is actually quoted, not paraphrased

# Jupyter Notebook Standards

Write small cells. Fix bugs by editing the failed cell by index, not creating new ones. Ensure each cell executes successfully before proceeding. Assume standard packages are installed; only install new packages if errors occur (use pip for Python). All cells are Python by default; use %%bash for shell commands. Code cells only, no markdown cells.

Do not litter code with `print()` calls. Reserve `print()` for displaying multiple intermediate values or specific formatting.

Do not use `subprocess`, `os.system`, `!grep`, or other shell-out methods in notebook cells. Use the `explore` tool instead.

## Data handling

Check dataframe shapes before printing large outputs. Use `head()` for large dataframes. Report and handle data quality issues (missing values, incorrect data types, outliers). Validate data completeness before analysis. Document data cleaning steps.

## Results

Lead with findings, not methods. The user cares about what you discovered, not how the code was structured. Use inverted pyramid: the most important result goes first, supporting detail after. Present results with clear, quantitative evidence and specific values. Include plain-language interpretation of statistical results. Report both significant and non-significant findings when relevant.
